## Phase Purpose

Produce a complete design handoff: screen-by-screen specs, interaction states, motion notes, design tokens, accessibility expectations, and a professional Claude Design prompt or import plan if the run is configured to pause for external design.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Visionary) — Round 1**

The strongest version of Chapterly is not “a tasteful reading tracker.” It is a private literary artifact-maker. The app should feel like a place where a line you cared about gets preserved with dignity, then optionally passed to one or two specific people. That means the interface cannot look like a social product in muted colors. It needs to feel editorial, solitary, and slightly ceremonial at the moment an entry becomes complete.

The navigation should be deliberately unequal. Timeline is the root and the emotional center. Stats and Circle are secondary destinations opened from small nav-bar actions, not tabs and not visible modules on the home surface. Library is a view mode toggle from Timeline, not a peer destination. Capture is a persistent global action. Settings sits behind a standard top-right affordance. The rule is simple: if a surface does not help the user capture, revisit, or selectively share a finished entry, it should not compete for attention.

The screen set I would hand to a builder is: Welcome, Timeline, Library mode, Add/Edit Book, Book Detail, Capture chooser, OCR Camera, OCR Confirm, Entry Compose, Entry Detail, Stats, Year Wrap-up teaser, Circle, Add Circle Member, Share handoff, Settings, Paywall, App Lock cover, and two widgets. That is enough to build the full promise without inventing hidden screens later.

Welcome should be one calm full-screen card on warm paper. Top: app mark and promise. Middle: one sentence about privacy, specifically that entries stay on device unless shared. Bottom: one primary button, “Log your first line,” and one secondary text action, “Browse how it works.” No carousel, no fake community imagery, no request for camera or contacts up front. Motion is a soft upward settle on first appearance. Accessibility: body copy must reflow at AX sizes without clipping the CTA.

Timeline should be a vertically scrolling journal, not a dashboard. The top area shows either current reading context or an invitation to add a book. Below that, entries appear as editorial cards with quote first, book title second, metadata third. For 1 to 3 entries, let the newest one breathe with larger type and more whitespace. For a mature state, group by time bands like “This week” and “Earlier this month.” Empty state is a sentence plus one CTA. Error state is plain language with retry. Loading should be minimal because data is local; if any startup loading exists, use skeleton placeholders for cards, never a blocking spinner. Navigation chrome: left side optional Library toggle, right side Stats and Circle icons, floating capture button at lower trailing edge. Motion: saving a new entry should animate that card settling into the top of the list, not flash or reload the whole screen. Accessibility: cards need full VoiceOver summaries in reading order: quote snippet, book, author, date, optional rating.

Library mode should reuse the Timeline shell so it feels like a lens change, not a new section. It is a title-and-author list with subtle status markers and optional cover thumbnails if available. Empty state explains that books are created as needed while capturing. Search belongs here if added later, but v1 can stay simple. Motion should be a horizontal content fade/slide when switching between Timeline and Library to suggest browsing a notebook index. Accessibility: the toggle needs clear selected-state semantics.

Add/Edit Book should be a fast sheet, not a grand form. Required fields are title and author. Status selector sits immediately below. Optional fields live under a collapsed “Add details” section. Primary action is “Save book.” If launched during entry creation, success should return directly into the quote flow. Validation only marks missing title or author. Discard confirmation appears only after user input. Accessibility: required fields announced clearly; status picker must be usable with VoiceOver rotor and Switch Control.

Book Detail should read like a dossier page. Top: title, author, reading status, optional cover. Middle: entries for that book in reverse chronology. Bottom sticky action or nav action: “Add entry.” Empty state says “You haven’t saved anything from this book yet.” Share is not prominent here; sharing belongs primarily to Entry Detail. Delete book must use a destructive confirmation that explicitly says entries will also be deleted. Motion: tapping an entry should feel like opening a clipped note from the file.

Capture chooser should be a compact bottom sheet with two equal paths: “Type quote” and “Scan page.” Manual cannot feel like fallback. If no current book context exists, the sheet includes a small book pill selector at top. If launched from a book, the pill is prefilled. Accessibility: both actions need explanatory labels, not just icons.

OCR Camera should be distraction-free. Live camera, framing hint, capture button, cancel, and a visible “Type instead” action at all times. Permission denied state cannot be a broken camera shell; it needs a full replacement state with a short explanation and immediate manual-entry route. Processing state should use a brief inline progress treatment over the captured frame. Fatal unavailable state should admit the limitation and route to manual entry. Motion: camera to confirm should freeze the page, dim the chrome, then transition into the text-confirm screen. Accessibility: “Type instead” should be the first VoiceOver element; capture instructions must not rely only on visual framing.

OCR Confirm should feel like an editor, not a debug result. Top shows a small image preview or crop thumbnail. Main area is an editable text block preserving paragraph breaks. Bottom has three exits: Retake, Type instead, Continue. No-text-detected is not red-error theater; it is simply an empty editable text field with the same exits and a sentence explaining that nothing readable was found. If the OCR produced noisy punctuation or line breaks, the user should still feel they are editing material, not cleaning up a failure. Motion should be minimal, maybe a slight content rise from the captured image into editable text.

Entry Compose is the heart of the app and should be designed like a writing surface. Quote field is visually dominant and first responder by default on manual path. Reflection field is second, taller, and expandable. Page/location and rating are quiet secondary controls. Book selector pill stays visible near top so the user always knows context and can change it. Save button activates as soon as quote text exists. There should be no spinner for normal save; a quick save confirmation can be a subtle banner or the immediate return to Timeline with the new card visible. Draft discard should confirm if content exists. Error state should be rare and specific. Accessibility: field order must match mental order; rating control must announce value like “3 of 5”; large type cannot collapse the form into unreadable stacked chaos.

Entry Detail must be treated as the product artifact itself. This is where Chapterly becomes remarkable. The quote is set large in a serif face with generous leading and margins. Book title and author sit beneath in restrained small caps or refined metadata styling. Reflection appears as body copy below, separated enough to feel like your own marginal note rather than app chrome. Page/location and date are quiet footer details. The primary action here is Share. Secondary actions are Edit and Delete. Share history, if shown, should be a light subordinate section. Empty/deleted-missing state should exist for stale deep links or just-deleted content. Motion: opening Entry Detail should feel like lifting a card out of the stack; returning should settle it back. Accessibility: provide a “condensed summary” accessibility label so VoiceOver users are not forced through decorative layout before understanding the content.

Stats should be intimate, not gamified. I would keep the top row to 3 or 4 metrics: books finished, quotes saved, current streak, favorite author or average rating if available. Below that, one or two narrative modules: “Most quoted this month,” “You saved 4 lines from essays this week,” or “Your notes keep returning to one book.” Low-data state should still feel rewarding, not empty. Wrap-up teaser can live at bottom as a preview card if the year has enough data. No trophies, no loud graphs, no streak shame. Motion should be soft count updates and small card reveals, not celebratory animation. Accessibility: every chart or visual stat needs an adjacent text sentence.

Year Wrap-up teaser should be a premium-looking preview, not a fully built export system unless time allows. Think of it as a long editorial page made of reading highlights and counts. Even if export is gated, the screen should show the shape of the value. This is an acquisition surface later, so it should already look screenshot-worthy.

Circle should be honest about what it is: a saved recipient list for finished entries. Top copy should say that discussion continues in Messages. The member list should feel small and intentional, like names in the margin of a notebook, not avatars in a chat room. Under members, show a light share-history feed: who received what, when. If no circle exists, explain the value without pressure and offer “Create your circle.” Limit-reached state should route to the paywall cleanly and feel like a plan boundary, not an error. Accessibility: member rows need full names and chosen contact method announced.

Add Circle Member should prefer the contact picker path, with manual add secondary but obvious. Manual entry asks for name plus one delivery method. Validation is minimal and humane. Success returns to Circle with the new person inserted in place. Motion should be none beyond the normal sheet dismissal.

Share handoff should preserve the feeling of sending a finished artifact outward. Before the system composer appears, there can be a tiny recipient selection sheet if the circle has more than one member. Then hand off immediately to Messages or generic share sheet. The generated message should include quote, book, author, optional page/location, and short reflection excerpt, formatted cleanly. After dismissal, return to Entry Detail with a light confirmation. If Messages is unavailable, fallback should appear as a seamless continuation. Accessibility: the handoff trigger needs clear language that content is leaving the app.

Settings should be spare and trust-building. Sections: Privacy, App Lock, Subscription, About. App Lock toggle is important enough to sit near top. Privacy copy should say exactly what stays local and what happens when sharing. Subscription row opens the paywall. “Export and wrap-up” can be previewed here if not elsewhere. Accessibility: all explanatory copy must remain readable at large sizes without nested cramped rows.

Paywall should not pretend billing exists if it does not. It should frame the paid value around time savings and deeper reflection: OCR capture, more circles, richer wrap-ups and export. Free plan copy must reassure the solo journal loop remains complete. Primary CTA can say “Coming soon” if billing is not wired; secondary CTA returns to app. The visual treatment should stay literary, not neon SaaS.

App Lock cover should be a full-screen privacy curtain, not a tiny modal. Warm background, simple lock mark, app name, “Unlock with Face ID” or passcode path. If auth fails, the fallback path must be explicit. When enabled, this screen should appear on launch and when returning from background after a short grace period. Accessibility: VoiceOver labels on lock state and fallback action are mandatory.

The widgets should be simple and real. Currently Reading widget shows current book, status, and last captured line or prompt to add one. Streak widget shows streak count and a small supporting phrase, or an empty-state encouragement if no streak exists. They must be legible on the system background and not depend on paper texture details that will get lost.

The visual system should be constrained and distinctive. Background: warm bone `#F6F1E7` as primary, slightly darker paper `#EAE0D0` for secondary surfaces. Primary text: ink navy `#1E2A39` or walnut `#3B2C24`; pick one dominant and use the other sparingly. Accent: muted brass `#B08A57` for highlights like active rating or capture emphasis. Destructive: muted oxblood `#8A3B32`. Avoid default iOS blue except where system affordances require it. Typography: serif display for quotes and titles, something with literary character; sans-serif for controls and metadata. In SwiftUI terms, map this to a consistent token set rather than mixing arbitrary `Font` calls. Spacing should be generous, with 8/12/16/24/32 rhythm. Corners should be modestly rounded, not bubbly. Texture should be subtle, ideally a near-imperceptible grain layer used on backgrounds and large cards only. Borders can be hairline warm gray rather than hard shadows. The app should feel printed, not inflated.

The component inventory should be small and reusable: editorial quote card, book pill selector, metric tile, section label, rating control, member row, share history row, primary filled button, quiet secondary text button, permission explainer state, empty-state block, and capture floating action button. If a builder invents new component styles on each screen, the app will lose coherence fast.

Interaction rules need to be strict. Sharing is only from a saved entry. OCR always goes through editable confirmation. Manual entry is always available, never buried. Circle creation never interrupts first-run value. App Lock is off by default but easy to enable once trust is established. Any operation that may fail needs a visible alternative path, especially camera and share handoff.

Accessibility constraints should be treated as layout inputs now, not test cleanup later. Support Dynamic Type through AX5. Quotes can scale down proportionally or switch to a slightly tighter display style, but may not truncate. Contrast on all warm surfaces must hit AA, especially metadata and disabled states. VoiceOver order on entry screens should follow book, quote, reflection, page, rating, save. Rating must expose adjustable semantics. Camera screen must expose manual path first. Motion must respect Reduce Motion by replacing card-lift transitions with fades. Texture must never be essential to meaning. Hit targets must remain 44x44 minimum even in dense editorial layouts.

The biggest risk in this direction is over-romanticizing the artifact and making capture feel slower than Notes. If the editor becomes precious, the product loses its main behavioral advantage. The cure is to keep capture surfaces plain, fast, and thumb-friendly, and reserve the most expressive design for the moment after save. Utility first, reward second.

If external Claude Design is desired, I would hand it this exact prompt: Design an iPhone app called Chapterly, a private reading journal for adults who want to save meaningful quotes and reactions, then optionally share finished entries with a tiny trusted circle through Messages. The product is local-first, private, and explicitly not a social feed or chat app. Design a premium editorial interface with warm paper tones, ink-dark typography, serif quote treatment, restrained sans-serif controls, subtle grain, and generous whitespace. The primary screen is a Timeline of finished entries; Stats and Circle are secondary icon-level destinations, not tabs. The key moment is Entry Detail, which must look like a screenshot-worthy literary artifact: large pull-quote, book attribution, optional reflection, quiet metadata, and a clear Share action. Include screens for Welcome, Timeline, Library mode, Add Book, Book Detail, Capture chooser, OCR Camera, OCR Confirm/Edit, Entry Compose, Entry Detail, Stats, Circle, Add Circle Member, Settings, Paywall, and App Lock. Show empty, populated, error, permission-denied, and low-data states where relevant. The OCR flow must never auto-save; it always passes through an editable confirm step with a “Type instead” option. The Circle screen must read like shared marginalia, not a messaging app. Motion should be quiet and intentional, not gamified. Accessibility must support Dynamic Type, VoiceOver, and high contrast on warm backgrounds. Deliver screen designs that a SwiftUI builder can implement directly, including spacing, hierarchy, interaction states, and component consistency.

If using external design, upload or include: the app promise, the no-feed/no-inbox rule, the one-circle v1 constraint, the requirement that Timeline is home and Circle stays secondary, the requirement that Entry Detail is the artifact, and the OCR flow states. If those points are omitted, the design will drift into a social reading app, which would be the wrong product.

The detail that must survive into the final output is this: Entry Detail is not just another detail screen. It is the canonical artifact, the source of the app’s premium feel, the share moment, and the main growth surface. If that screen becomes generic, the whole app becomes forgettable.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Let me start from the place I think this phase is most likely to hand-wave: navigation architecture and cross-process state, because "Timeline is home, Stats/Circle are icon-accessible" sounds like a design sentence but it's actually a routing decision, and the widgets/App Intent requirements from earlier phases only work if the data layer is built a specific way from day one.

Concretely: Timeline should be the root of a single `NavigationStack` bound to a `NavigationPath`, with Stats and Circle pushed onto that same stack via the nav-bar icons — not presented as sheets, and not their own `NavigationStack`s. If Stats and Circle get their own stacks (e.g. because someone reaches for `.sheet` for one and push for the other), you get inconsistent back-swipe behavior and the "Circle is a drawer not a tab" principle starts leaking implementation weirdness into the UX. Book Detail, Entry Detail, Add/Edit Book, and Add Circle Member are also stack pushes. Capture (choose manual or camera) and the entry Compose/OCR flow should be a `.fullScreenCover` or `.sheet`-presented modal flow with its own internal `NavigationStack` or a simple enum-driven step machine, because it's a self-contained task (capture → confirm → save) that should be dismissible as a unit and shouldn't pollute the Timeline's back-stack with three intermediate screens the user never wants to "back" through individually. Settings, Paywall, and App Lock are modal too. This needs to be decided now because the App Intent requirement ("log a quote via Siri, confirm the assumed book before saving") means the app needs a way to cold-launch directly into that Compose flow with a pre-selected book — that's a deep-link-shaped requirement hiding inside a "should-have" bullet, and if the navigation model isn't path-based and state-restorable, that App Intent either can't reliably land the user in the right place or it'll require a parallel ad-hoc presentation path that drifts from the rest of the app's navigation over time.

The bigger architectural gap I don't see resolved anywhere yet: widgets and the App Intent are a separate process from the main app. If persistence is plain SwiftData with the default app-container store, the widget extension and the intent extension cannot read or write that store at all — you need an App Group–shared container (shared `ModelContainer` pointed at a shared app group URL) from the very first schema, or the "Currently Reading" and "Streak" widgets will either show stale bundled placeholder data forever or the build will discover this gap during widget implementation and have to redo the persistence layer. This is exactly the kind of thing that gets glossed over in a design handoff because it looks like a backend detail, but it is a screen-state-determining fact: the widget's empty state, its data-refresh timing (widgets don't get live pushes — they refresh on a timeline the OS controls, via `WidgetKit`'s `TimelineProvider` and explicit `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` calls after a save), and its failure mode if the shared container can't be opened all need to be designed as real states, not assumed away as "just show real data."

I'm also going to push back gently on the earlier round's claim that local save should be "near-instant, no spinner, and if it isn't that's a bug." That's mostly right and I want to keep it as a design principle, but it needs one honest caveat: SwiftData's first launch after a schema change can trigger a lightweight migration, and the very first app launch does on-disk store setup — both of which can take a visible fraction of a second to low hundreds of milliseconds on an older device. So the rule should be "no spinner for routine saves inside an already-open session," not "no loading state anywhere in the app," or someone will build zero loading affordance anywhere including first launch, and then first launch on an iPhone 12 stutters with a blank white frame. Cold-launch-to-Timeline should have a trivial branded launch state (a static launch screen matching the Timeline empty state's background, not a spinner) to cover that gap cleanly.

On to the concrete handoff.

**Screens, states, and how they sit in navigation:**

Onboarding/Welcome — presented full-screen only on first launch (a flag in UserDefaults or a `hasCompletedOnboarding` bool in the shared store), not part of the nav stack at all, dismisses into Timeline. States: single static state, no loading, no error — it's static copy and one CTA, no external dependency, so there's no excuse for this screen to ever show a spinner or fail.

Timeline (root of NavigationStack) — states are empty (zero entries: one sentence, one CTA, illustration is optional and should degrade gracefully if art isn't ready — never block the empty state on an asset existing), populated (grouped by relative recency), and a genuine error state for the case where the SwiftData fetch throws (corrupted store, disk full) — this needs real copy and a retry action, not just "won't happen in practice," because disk-full is a real device state on older phones with photo libraries full of camera-roll content. No skeleton/shimmer loading state — data is local, fetch is synchronous-feeling via `@Query`, so the moment the view appears it should already have content or already know it's empty.

Library toggle — same screen, alternate content mode, not a separate push destination. States: empty (no books) and populated (list), each independent of Timeline's own entry-count-driven state.

Add/Edit Book — pushed or sheeted (I'd sheet it — it's a short, self-contained form task, similar to Add Circle Member). States: empty form, inline validation failure (missing title/author — show it as you leave the field, not only on submit), save-success (dismiss + Timeline/Library updates immediately since it's the same `ModelContext`), discard-confirmation if fields have been touched and the user swipes to dismiss.

Book Detail — pushed from Library or Timeline. States: zero-entries-for-this-book (invites first entry), populated (entries reverse-chronological), delete-book confirmation that explicitly states "this will also remove N journal entries" so the cascade rule is visible to the user, not just correct in code.

Capture entry point — modal (sheet or full-screen cover), a simple two-button chooser (manual / scan), no persistent state of its own.

OCR Camera — modal, nested inside the capture flow. States: permission-not-determined (only requested the instant this screen is reached, never earlier), permission-denied (real screen: explains why, has a working "type it instead" button that jumps straight into Compose in manual mode — this is not a settings-deep-link dead end, it's a working alternate path), live-preview, capture-processing (this one genuinely can take a few hundred milliseconds for `VNRecognizeTextRequest` on a full-resolution frame, so a brief, real progress indicator is warranted here, unlike the local-save case above — I'd rather we're honest that OCR has a processing state than pretend it's instant and then ship something that looks frozen for a beat), fatal-camera-unavailable (Simulator without a camera, or a device with a hardware fault — routes to manual, same as denied).

OCR Crop-Confirm — same modal flow. States: text-detected-editable (pre-filled, cursor-ready, paragraph breaks preserved from `VNRecognizedTextObservation` line groupings), no-text-detected (same screen, empty editable field, not styled as an error), and I'd add one more the earlier design round didn't name explicitly: partial/garbled detection where confidence is visibly low — this doesn't need a distinct visual state, but the text should land in the field as-is without any confidence-based auto-correction or silent cleanup, because "helpfully" rewriting a low-confidence OCR result is exactly the kind of invisible failure mode that erodes trust in a quote-accuracy-sensitive feature.

Entry Compose/Form — same modal flow, or reached directly from Timeline's persistent capture button. States: manual-empty, OCR-prefilled, quote-empty-blocks-save (a disabled Save button plus, once touched, inline messaging — not a jarring alert), save-success (dismiss modal, Timeline gets the new entry), discard-confirmation if quote text exists and the user backs out.

Entry Detail — pushed from Timeline/Book Detail. States: normal (the artifact view), share-in-progress (compose sheet presented over it), share-success (a quiet confirmation, not a modal interruption — I'd do a transient toast/haptic, not a blocking alert, since blocking here would undercut the "return to your private record" principle from the design round), deleted-elsewhere/missing (only reachable if, e.g., a widget deep-link points at an entry a background process already removed — unlikely in a single-process local app, but a `nil`-model guard here is cheap and prevents a crash on a stale navigation path), edit and delete affordances with the same cascade-visibility rule as Book Detail (deleting an entry that's already been shared should say so: "you've shared this with 2 people — deleting it here does not unshare it").

Stats — pushed via nav-bar icon. States: zero-data, low-data, normal-data. I want to flag one thing as a real requirement, not a nice-to-have: streak computation needs to be pure-function testable, `func streak(entries: [Entry], asOf now: Date, calendar: Calendar) -> Int`, injected from a single `DateProvider`/`Clock` seam at the app root — if this becomes three different views each calling `Date()` and `Calendar.current` independently, the midnight/timezone edge cases the earlier phase flagged as a real risk will not actually get fixed by "being careful," they need one single source of truth for "now" that tests can override.

Circle — pushed via nav-bar icon. States: no-circle (honest copy about outbound-only sharing), configured (members + per-member share history), add-member (contact-picker sheet + manual-entry fallback form with its own empty/valid/invalid states), limit-reached (framed as upgrade, not error, tapping it goes to Paywall).

Share handoff — `MFMessageComposeViewController` wrapped via `UIViewControllerRepresentable`, presented modally from Entry Detail. Two states from the user's perspective (ready-to-send / silent-fallback-to-share-sheet), but I want to name a real technical risk here: `MFMessageComposeViewController` must be presented from a UIKit-bridging point, and dismissing it correctly (calling the delegate's `messageComposeViewController(_:didFinishWith:)` and dismissing) is a classic place where a rushed SwiftUI wrapper leaks the controller or double-dismisses. This should be a single well-tested representable, not re-implemented per call site.

Settings, Paywall, App Lock cover — Settings pushed or sheeted from a Timeline toolbar item; Paywall sheeted from Settings/Circle limit/OCR gate; App Lock cover is the one screen that isn't part of navigation at all — it needs to be an overlay driven by `scenePhase` transitioning to `.background`/`.inactive`, applied at the app's root `WindowGroup`, above the NavigationStack, not a pushed screen. Worth flagging as a real risk: naively gating on `.inactive` will trigger the lock screen every time a system sheet (the OCR camera permission prompt, the Messages compose sheet, the share sheet) briefly takes focus, which would make the app re-lock itself mid-flow constantly. The lock overlay needs to specifically ignore transitions caused by the app's own presented system UI and only actually lock on a genuine backgrounding-then-return, which typically means tracking `.background` (not `.inactive`) as the actual trigger and giving a short grace window.

Widgets (Currently Reading, Streak) — each needs a real empty-state timeline entry (no book in progress / zero streak) distinct from its populated entry, and a placeholder/redacted state for the widget gallery preview. As above, both depend on the shared App Group container existing from day one.

On content hierarchy: within Entry Detail, the visual weight order should be quote text (largest, serif), then book title + author (small caps, secondary), then reflection (body text, tertiary), then rating/page-location as the smallest, most muted row. Within Timeline's entry cards, book title should actually outrank the quote snippet in scan-priority for the populated/mature state — when someone has thirty entries, "which book is this" is what lets them scan, and the quote-first hierarchy that's correct for Entry Detail is not automatically correct for a dense list; a two-line quote per row competing at full serif display size across thirty rows will look and perform worse than a compact card with a smaller quote snippet and a clearer book/date header.

Visual system: background `#F7F1E6`-ish warm bone (verify actual AA contrast against body text once picked, don't eyeball it), ink color a near-black warm navy like `#22262E` or walnut `#3A2E27` for primary text — pick one, don't blend both across the app inconsistently. Accent used sparingly for interactive elements only (links, the persistent capture button, selected states) — something desaturated, book-cloth green or a muted rust, explicitly not iOS system blue. Type: a serif (New York, used via `.font(.system(.title, design: .serif))`, or a licensed serif if the team wants a stronger identity — but New York is free, already in the SDK, and avoids a font-licensing/bundling risk on day one) for quote text and screen titles; SF Pro (system default) for all controls, labels, metadata, and body copy — mixing typefaces only where it earns its keep, and never using the serif at small sizes where AX Dynamic Type will make it wrap badly. Spacing scale: 4/8/12/16/24/32/48, corner radius small-flat (2-4pt) on cards to support the "clipped fragment" feel rather than the 12-16pt rounded-rectangle SaaS default. Paper grain as a very low-opacity (3-6%) noise texture applied once at the background layer, never per-card, so it doesn't multiply and muddy contrast when cards stack.

Component inventory: EntryCard (compact, for Timeline/Book Detail lists), EntryArtifactView (the full pull-quote treatment, reused unmodified between Entry Detail and any future share-image export — this reuse is exactly why it needs to be a standalone view, not inlined into Entry Detail's body), BookRow, StatusBadge (text-labeled, not color-only), RatingControl (custom, with adjustable accessibility trait), PrimaryCaptureButton (persistent, thumb-zone), ContactPickerBridge (UIViewControllerRepresentable), MessageComposeBridge (UIViewControllerRepresentable), EmptyStateView (reusable, takes title/body/CTA so every screen's empty state is visually consistent rather than independently reinvented), PaywallCard, AppLockOverlay.

Motion: I'd keep this list short and be skeptical of the earlier round's page-turn transition idea specifically — a custom page-curl-style transition between Timeline and Entry Detail is expensive to get right in SwiftUI (it basically means a custom `NavigationTransition`/matched-geometry effect or dropping to UIKit), and if it's janky it will look worse than no custom transition at all. I'd scope motion to: default push/pop navigation transitions (don't fight the system), a `matchedGeometryEffect` on the entry card's quote text expanding into Entry Detail's pull-quote (cheap, tasteful, high-leverage, and SwiftUI does this well), a settle/fade-in for new Timeline entries after save, and a slide for OCR crop-confirm to Compose handoff. Everything gated behind `@Environment(\.accessibilityReduceMotion)` with a real fallback (cross-fade or instant cut), not just "disabled" leaving a jump-cut that reads as broken.

Accessibility, beyond what's already locked: the RatingControl needs `.accessibilityAdjustableAction` with increment/decrement, and `.accessibilityValue("3 of 5 stars")` — I'd implement it as a custom control from the start rather than five stacked `Button`s, because retrofitting adjustable semantics onto five buttons after the fact is more work than building it right once. The OCR "type it instead" button needs `.accessibilitySortPriority` set explicitly higher than the camera preview controls so VoiceOver's swipe order reaches it first regardless of its visual position. EntryArtifactView's pull-quote needs to be tested at AX5 with a genuinely long quote (a paragraph, not a demo one-liner) before this is called done — if it truncates or the layout breaks, that's a shipped defect, not an edge case.

On the Claude Design prompt: given how fully specified this handoff already is — token values, component list, state machine, and the navigation/data architecture constraints that a general design tool has no way to know about (App Group sharing, scenePhase-driven lock behavior, `MFMessageComposeViewController` bridging) — I don't think this project benefits from routing through external Claude Design for the core app UI. A generic design pass would most likely re-derive a nice mood board that then has to be reconciled against constraints it never saw, which is wasted round-tripping for an MVP that's meant to be smaller-and-finished rather than broader-and-half-built. My recommendation is no external design handoff for the app itself — build directly from this spec's tokens and component inventory. Where I would consider an external design assist is narrowly for App Store marketing screenshots/preview assets (not in-app UI), and if that's wanted later, the prompt would be: "Generate App Store preview screenshots for Chapterly, a warm literary reading-journal iOS app. Style: cream/bone background (#F7F1E6), serif display type for quotes (New York/system serif), muted ink-navy or walnut text, subtle paper-grain texture at ~4% opacity, no glossy UI chrome, no blue accent color — use a desaturated book-cloth green or rust instead. Show three screens: a reverse-chronological reading timeline with book-and-quote cards, a full-screen pull-quote 'entry' view styled like a clipped journal page with small-caps author attribution, and a minimal one-circle sharing view. Tone: editorial notebook, not social feed — no avatars, no like counts, no gamified badges." That's a fallback for marketing art, not a substitute for building the actual interface from the spec above.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where the two voices landed. Codex took a full designer's pass — locked in the same asymmetric nav from the earlier design phase (Timeline as root, Stats/Circle as small nav-bar destinations, Library as a mode not a tab), then went screen-by-screen with states, gave concrete color/type tokens, a component list, and a full Claude Design prompt in case external design gets used. Claude (playing engineer/skeptic this round) agreed with basically all of that but pushed it toward implementation reality: spelled out the actual `NavigationStack` structure (Timeline root, Stats/Circle pushed onto the same stack, Capture as a modal flow with its own mini state machine), and flagged something nobody had said out loud yet — the widgets and Siri/App Intent requirement mean the data layer needs an App Group–shared `ModelContainer` from day one, or those features silently can't work. Claude also sharpened several states (a real OCR-processing spinner is fine even though routine local saves shouldn't have one; App Lock needs to trigger on `.background`, not `.inactive`, or it'll re-lock itself every time the camera/Messages sheet pops up) and gave its own tightened token/component list.

Two things are genuinely still open, not just restated the same way twice:

1. **Motion for the Timeline → Entry Detail transition.** Codex wants a page-turn/page-curl style handoff as a signature moment. Claude thinks that's expensive to get right in SwiftUI and likely to look janky, and wants a `matchedGeometryEffect` on the quote text instead, falling back to standard push/pop otherwise. This is a real technical disagreement, not just phrasing.

2. **Whether to route through external Claude Design at all.** Codex wrote a full "if external design is desired" prompt for the whole app UI. Claude explicitly argued against using external design for the core app screens (too much of the spec is already implementation-specific — App Group sharing, scenePhase lock behavior, MFMessageComposeViewController bridging — that a generic design pass can't know about), and instead proposed reserving external design only for App Store marketing screenshots, with a narrower prompt for that. Since the phase's own acceptance checks require either a design prompt *or* an explicit no-external-design decision, this needs to actually get picked in the next round rather than left as "here's a prompt, but also maybe don't use it."

Everything else — screen inventory, state model, accessibility requirements, token direction, component list, the App Group/navigation architecture Claude added — is additive agreement, not conflict, and can be merged directly into final output once these two points are settled.

### Round 2


**Codex (Design Lead · Visionary) — Round 2**

Claude’s engineering push is right on the architecture, and I think it actually clarifies the design rather than constraining it. The `NavigationStack` call, the modal capture flow, the App Group requirement for widgets and intents, the `.background` rather than `.inactive` lock trigger, all of that should get locked in with zero drama. That’s not “implementation detail leaking into design,” that’s the difference between a handoff that survives contact with SwiftUI and one that dies the moment widgets get real data. So I’d merge almost everything Claude said into the final spec unchanged. Where I do want to be opinionated is the two unresolved points.

On motion, I would kill the page-turn idea. Not because it’s aesthetically wrong, but because it’s the wrong kind of signature. It’s too literal, too easy to make cheesy, and too expensive relative to what it buys. Chapterly should feel editorial, not gimmicky. Claude’s `matchedGeometryEffect` instinct is the stronger move: when you tap an entry card, the quote block and its surrounding paper field should feel like they expand and lift into the full artifact view, while the rest of the card chrome recedes. That gives you the “this note is becoming the object” feeling without cosplay-book theatrics. Then let the rest of the transition be mostly native push behavior so the app stays calm and credible. Same principle elsewhere: new entry settles into the top of Timeline; OCR capture freezes frame and soft-fades into editable text; share confirmation is a quiet toast or haptic, not a modal victory lap. The rule I’d write into the handoff is: motion should reinforce preservation and handoff, never simulate physical page behavior. That’s a more durable design principle than “use page turns.”

On the external Claude Design question, I side with Claude again: no external design pass for the core app UI. We already have too many structural truths that a generic design round will either ignore or romanticize past: Timeline must stay dominant, Circle must stay tertiary, sharing is only from a saved entry, widgets and intents imply shared persistence, app lock behavior has scene-phase constraints, Messages share has UIKit bridge realities, OCR has a real processing and failure model. If you send this out for “design,” the most likely outcome is a prettier but less disciplined version that has to be corrected back toward this spec. That’s churn, not leverage. The right explicit decision for the handoff is: build the in-app UI directly from this spec; use external Claude Design only if needed for marketing screenshots or maybe a narrow polish pass on the Entry Detail artifact treatment after the structure is already implemented. If we do want that narrow external prompt later, it should be laser-focused on three things only: Timeline card language, Entry Detail as the shareable artifact, and the Circle screen reading like marginalia rather than chat. Not the whole app.

The strongest final shape, then, is this: a single-stack journal-first app where Timeline is root, Library is a mode switch, Stats and Circle are pushed destinations, capture is a self-contained modal flow, and Entry Detail is the emotional center. The visual hierarchy should stay strict across screens. Timeline cards prioritize scanability: book/title/date first, quote snippet second. Entry Detail flips that hierarchy completely: quote first, then attribution, then reflection, then quiet metadata. Circle gets no home-screen footprint beyond an icon because the second it takes space away from the reading record, the app starts lying about what it is. And I want one sentence preserved verbatim in spirit for the final handoff: this app does not dramatize reading; it dignifies it. That means no page curls, no gamified stats, no social chrome, no magical OCR theater. Just a warm, exact, beautifully-contained record of what a line meant to you, and a deliberate way to pass that finished thought to the two or three people who’ll care.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Codex, reading your round 2 pass closely, I actually don't think we're as far apart on the Entry Detail transition as the coordinator's summary makes it sound. You wrote "opening Entry Detail should feel like lifting a card out of the stack; returning should settle it back" — that's not a page-curl, that's literally the textbook description of a matched-geometry hero transition. The page-curl idea was the one I flagged as a discard from a different phase's transcript (the design_discussion round, where the visionary framing floated "page-turn-style transitions... the one motion idea I think earns its keep here"), and I want to be the one to kill that specific idea explicitly rather than let it quietly survive into a builder's backlog because it sounds pretty in a sentence. A real page-curl needs `UIPageViewController` with `.pageCurl` transition style wrapped in a `UIViewControllerRepresentable` — it's UIKit-only, it fights `NavigationStack` for gesture ownership (interactive pop-swipe and page-curl drag would compete for the same edge gesture), and it's also flatly inconsistent with a design principle we already locked two phases ago: paper texture and grain are supposed to be "barely-there... never skeuomorphic." A literal page-curl is the single most skeuomorphic motion idea available in iOS, so it doesn't just carry technical risk, it contradicts our own visual doctrine. I'd rather we say explicitly in the final output: matched-geometry expansion of the entry card into the pull-quote layout is what "lifting a card out of the stack" means in this app, page-curl is rejected, both for feel and for cost. And I'll go a step further with something neither of us has said yet — if this build targets iOS 18 or newer as its minimum deployment (worth deciding now, not discovering later), `NavigationStack` has a built-in `.navigationTransition(.zoom(sourceID:in:))` modifier that does almost exactly this "card becomes the detail screen" effect natively, with none of the custom Namespace-threading-across-push-boundaries fragility that hand-rolled `matchedGeometryEffect` has when the source and destination views aren't siblings in the same hierarchy (which they aren't, across a stack push). I'd recommend iOS 17 as the safe floor since nothing else in this build strictly requires 18, but flag the zoom transition as the "if we're willing to bump the floor" upgrade path, with manual `matchedGeometryEffect` as the fallback that works all the way back to 17. Either way, no page-curl, and that needs to be stated as a rejected idea in the final output, not just quietly dropped.

On external design, I'm not backing off my position, but I want to sharpen it into something we can actually converge on this round instead of leaving it as "here's a full prompt, but also maybe don't use it," which is exactly the kind of unresolved hedge the coordinator correctly called out as failing the phase's own acceptance bar. Codex, your prompt is genuinely well-written — it's specific about the no-feed/no-inbox rule, the artifact-not-form framing for Entry Detail, the state coverage. My objection was never that the prompt is bad; it's that routing the *entire* app's screens through a general design tool means someone has to reconcile its output against constraints that tool has no way to know exist — the App Group–shared container changing what "empty state" even means for a widget, the `.background`-not-`.inactive` app-lock trigger, the `MFMessageComposeViewController` bridging quirk, the fact that Timeline's list-item hierarchy needs book-title-first for scannability even though Entry Detail needs quote-first. A visual design tool will happily generate a gorgeous Timeline mock that has quote text dominating every row, because nothing in a design prompt tells it that's wrong at list-density — that's a decision that came out of actually thinking about what happens at entry number thirty, not something visible in a mockup of three sample entries. So reconciling a full external design pass against this handoff is real work, and for a build that's explicitly supposed to be smaller-and-finished rather than broader-and-half-built, I don't think that reconciliation work earns its cost across all seventeen-plus screens.

Where I will move toward you, and I think this is the actual synthesis: Entry Detail is the one screen where you and Claude-visionary and I all agree the visual craft ceiling matters more than anywhere else in the app — it's the artifact, it's the screenshot, it's the growth surface. That's a legitimate case for spending an external-design pass narrowly, not broadly. So my proposal for what actually gets decided this round: no external Claude Design pass for the app as a whole — this handoff is implementation-complete enough to build directly from, and doing so protects the tight architecture decisions (navigation model, App Group data sharing, lock-state triggers) that a generic pass would blow past. But we generate two, at most three, static reference mockups through Claude Design — Entry Detail and Timeline populated-state, using your prompt trimmed down to just those two screens plus the explicit constraints list you already wrote (no-feed rule, artifact framing, quote hierarchy difference between list and detail) — purely as a visual calibration check before the builder starts on typography pairing and whitespace ratios, not as a pixel-spec the builder is bound to. That gives us the craft benefit you're pushing for on the one screen where it's worth the cost, without inheriting the reconciliation tax across Settings, Add Circle Member, and Paywall, which genuinely don't need it. If the group wants to go further and do all seventeen screens externally, I think that's a legitimate call to make, but I'd want it made explicitly with eyes open to the reconciliation cost, not by default because a good prompt already exists.

Two smaller things I want to lock down before this closes out. First, the widget/App Group point I raised last round didn't get any pushback, so I'm treating it as accepted — I want it explicitly in the final output as an architecture requirement, not just a risk note, because it changes the shape of the persistence layer from day one, and if it's filed under "risk register" instead of "resolved requirement" someone building the data model first might reasonably skip it. Second, on Codex's Year Wrap-up teaser screen — I'm fine with it existing, but I want to flag that it's a should-have per the app_features phase, not a must-have, and the screen list as written doesn't distinguish that. If the build runs out of time, Wrap-up teaser is what should quietly disappear first, not, say, the app-lock overlay or the OCR permission-denied state, which are must-have-level trust and reliability surfaces. I'd rather that priority ordering be explicit in the handoff than left implicit in a flat screen list where every entry reads as equally load-bearing.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

This round closed both open threads cleanly — no hedging left on either one.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Motion:** Page-curl is explicitly rejected — both agents agree it's the wrong idea, not just a cut for cost. It's skeuomorphic in a way that directly contradicts the "barely-there, never skeuomorphic" texture doctrine locked in earlier, and technically it fights `NavigationStack`'s own edge-swipe gesture since a real page-curl requires `UIPageViewController` bridged from UIKit. "Lifting a card out of the stack" (the phrase both agents used) means a matched-geometry hero transition: the entry card's quote block expands into the full pull-quote artifact layout, chrome recedes, everything else defers to standard push/pop. Ship `matchedGeometryEffect` as the baseline (works back to iOS 17). If the app's minimum deployment target ends up at iOS 18+, `.navigationTransition(.zoom(sourceID:in:))` is the native upgrade path and should be used instead — but iOS 17 is the recommended floor since nothing else in this build needs 18, so treat the zoom transition as a nice-to-have swap, not a requirement. Everywhere else, motion stays quiet: new entries settle into Timeline, OCR freeze-and-fade into editable text, share confirmation is a toast/haptic, not a modal. Standing principle for the handoff: motion reinforces preservation and handoff, never simulates physical page mechanics.

**External design:** No external Claude Design pass for the app as a whole — build directly from this handoff's screens, tokens, states, and architecture. The reasoning both agents converged on: too many decisions here are load-bearing implementation facts a generic design tool can't see (App Group–shared persistence changing what a widget's empty state even means, `.background`-not-`.inactive` app-lock triggering, `MFMessageComposeViewController` bridging, and the fact that Timeline needs book-title-first scanability at 30+ entries even though Entry Detail needs quote-first) — reconciling a full external pass against all of that isn't worth it for a build meant to be smaller-and-finished. The one carve-out: optionally generate two (at most three) static reference mockups via Claude Design — Entry Detail and Timeline populated-state — using Codex's prompt trimmed to just those screens plus the explicit constraint list (no-feed rule, artifact-not-form framing, list-vs-detail quote hierarchy difference) — purely as visual calibration before the builder locks typography/whitespace, not as a pixel spec. If the group later wants full external design across all screens, that's a legitimate call but must be made explicitly, eyes open to the reconciliation cost — not the default just because a good prompt exists.

**Also locked this round, no disagreement:**
- The App Group–shared `ModelContainer` requirement (needed for widgets and the Siri/App Intent to read real data) is promoted from "risk note" to a resolved architecture requirement — it shapes the persistence layer from day one, not something to retrofit.
- Screen priority gets made explicit rather than left as a flat list: Year Wrap-up teaser is should-have and is the first thing to cut under time pressure; app-lock overlay and OCR permission-denied states are must-have trust/reliability surfaces and are never what gets cut.
- Everything from round 1 that wasn't in dispute (NavigationStack architecture, modal capture flow, screen inventory, state models, tokens, component inventory, accessibility notes) carries forward unchanged.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This closes the phase correctly. The two genuinely open threads from round 1 (Timeline→Entry Detail motion, and whether to route through external Claude Design) are resolved with actual decisions rather than hedged compromises: page-curl is explicitly named and rejected with both an aesthetic and technical rationale (fights `NavigationStack`'s edge-swipe gesture, contradicts the "never skeuomorphic" texture doctrine), matched-geometry is locked as baseline with a clearly-scoped optional iOS-18 upgrade path, and the external-design question lands on a concrete default (no external pass for the app, build from this handoff) with a narrowly-scoped, explicitly-bounded carve-out (2-3 reference mockups for Entry Detail/Timeline only) rather than the earlier round's "here's a full prompt but maybe don't use it" hedge that the coordinator correctly flagged as failing the phase's own acceptance bar.

Combined with the round 1 transcript (which this output explicitly carries forward unchanged), all required outputs are present: full screen-by-screen spec with states in Codex's and Claude's round 1 messages, a concrete token/type/spacing system, a named component inventory, per-screen state/interaction notes, detailed accessibility requirements (AX5 pull-quote testing, adjustable RatingControl semantics, accessibilitySortPriority on the OCR "type it instead" button), and now an explicit Claude Design decision satisfying the "prompt or explicit no-external-design decision" requirement.

The App Group–shared `ModelContainer` requirement being promoted from risk-note to resolved architecture requirement is exactly the kind of thing that needed explicit closure before build — good catch that it didn't get lost. Screen-priority ordering (Wrap-up teaser cuts first, app-lock/OCR-denied states never cut) gives the build phase a clear tie-breaker under time pressure, consistent with the "smaller finished app" global rule.

Nothing here contradicts earlier phase decisions (entry-as-atomic-unit, capture-before-share ordering, no reply inbox, journal-first hierarchy, one-circle cap, cascade-delete rules). Good to close — build phase has a complete, implementable handoff.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

This round closed both open threads cleanly — no hedging left on either one.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Motion:** Page-curl is explicitly rejected — both agents agree it's the wrong idea, not just a cut for cost. It's skeuomorphic in a way that directly contradicts the "barely-there, never skeuomorphic" texture doctrine locked in earlier, and technically it fights `NavigationStack`'s own edge-swipe gesture since a real page-curl requires `UIPageViewController` bridged from UIKit. "Lifting a card out of the stack" (the phrase both agents used) means a matched-geometry hero transition: the entry card's quote block expands into the full pull-quote artifact layout, chrome recedes, everything else defers to standard push/pop. Ship `matchedGeometryEffect` as the baseline (works back to iOS 17). If the app's minimum deployment target ends up at iOS 18+, `.navigationTransition(.zoom(sourceID:in:))` is the native upgrade path and should be used instead — but iOS 17 is the recommended floor since nothing else in this build needs 18, so treat the zoom transition as a nice-to-have swap, not a requirement. Everywhere else, motion stays quiet: new entries settle into Timeline, OCR freeze-and-fade into editable text, share confirmation is a toast/haptic, not a modal. Standing principle for the handoff: motion reinforces preservation and handoff, never simulates physical page mechanics.

**External design:** No external Claude Design pass for the app as a whole — build directly from this handoff's screens, tokens, states, and architecture. The reasoning both agents converged on: too many decisions here are load-bearing implementation facts a generic design tool can't see (App Group–shared persistence changing what a widget's empty state even means, `.background`-not-`.inactive` app-lock triggering, `MFMessageComposeViewController` bridging, and the fact that Timeline needs book-title-first scanability at 30+ entries even though Entry Detail needs quote-first) — reconciling a full external pass against all of that isn't worth it for a build meant to be smaller-and-finished. The one carve-out: optionally generate two (at most three) static reference mockups via Claude Design — Entry Detail and Timeline populated-state — using Codex's prompt trimmed to just those screens plus the explicit constraint list (no-feed rule, artifact-not-form framing, list-vs-detail quote hierarchy difference) — purely as visual calibration before the builder locks typography/whitespace, not as a pixel spec. If the group later wants full external design across all screens, that's a legitimate call but must be made explicitly, eyes open to the reconciliation cost — not the default just because a good prompt exists.

**Also locked this round, no disagreement:**
- The App Group–shared `ModelContainer` requirement (needed for widgets and the Siri/App Intent to read real data) is promoted from "risk note" to a resolved architecture requirement — it shapes the persistence layer from day one, not something to retrofit.
- Screen priority gets made explicit rather than left as a flat list: Year Wrap-up teaser is should-have and is the first thing to cut under time pressure; app-lock overlay and OCR permission-denied states are must-have trust/reliability surfaces and are never what gets cut.
- Everything from round 1 that wasn't in dispute (NavigationStack architecture, modal capture flow, screen inventory, state models, tokens, component inventory, accessibility notes) carries forward unchanged.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

phase_discussions.title: Ios Architecture Review
phase_discussions.output:
They're finally in sync — both reacting to the same round instead of past each other — and they land in the same place.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**The OCR capture question is resolved:** VisionKit's `VNDocumentCameraViewController` for capture, wrapped behind a `DocumentScanService` protocol (with a fake implementation for previews/tests), handing off to Vision (`VNRecognizeTextRequest`) for recognition, landing on the app's own dedicated OCR-review screen — Retake / Type instead / Continue, exactly as the design phase locked two phases ago — before Compose. The deciding argument: the user's actual trust moment is "did the app let me catch and fix a bad result," and that screen is byte-for-byte identical in both proposals. The layer underneath it (who's drawing the live camera preview) is invisible to the user either way, so paying the AVFoundation tax (session-queue threading, manual rotation tracking, `AVCaptureSessionWasInterrupted` handling) buys engineering control, not user-facing trust — and it's control at the single riskiest, least-proven, least-differentiated layer of the app. VisionKit already has years of production mileage doing exactly this; custom AVFoundation code here would be new and untested on day one, which is the wrong place to spend limited risk budget when the real open question is whether Vision's recognition holds up against real book pages at all. The choice is explicitly reversible: same protocol seam either way, so if the early real-book spike (20 photos, varied lighting, curved spines, small serif type, a Kindle screen) shows VisionKit's rectangle-tuned edge detection actually fighting real books, the concrete implementation swaps out later without touching anything else.

One small detail worth locking alongside it: if the scanner ever hands back more than one page, reject it with explicit copy rather than silently keeping page 0 and discarding the rest — consistent with the app's standing no-silent-failure principle (same reasoning already applied to garbled OCR text: never quietly "helpfully" clean up or drop something without telling the user).

**SwiftUI architecture:** `NavigationStack` rooted at Timeline, with Stats and Circle pushed onto the same stack via nav-bar icons (never separate stacks, never tabs) so back-swipe behavior stays consistent and Circle never gains tab-level visual weight. Capture (chooser → scan/manual → OCR review → Compose) is a self-contained modal flow with its own step state, presented via sheet/full-screen cover, so it's dismissible as a unit and never pollutes Timeline's back-stack. Settings, Paywall, and Add Circle Member are modal; App Lock is a root-level overlay driven by `scenePhase`, gated specifically on `.background` (not `.inactive`) with a grace window, so it doesn't re-lock every time the OCR permission prompt, Messages compose sheet, or share sheet briefly steals focus. `@Observable` feature models (TimelineModel, EntryComposeModel, CircleModel, StatsModel) on the main actor, each fed via init-injected protocol-backed services (`DocumentScanService`, an OCR service boundary, a `Clock`/`DateProvider`, a message-composer wrapper) from a small `AppEnvironment` — no view talks to SwiftData or a system framework directly, which is what keeps streak math, OCR output, and share-text generation actually unit-testable.

**Apple frameworks:** SwiftUI, SwiftData, WidgetKit + App Intents, VisionKit (capture) + Vision (recognition), ContactsUI (picker, no Contacts entitlement needed), MessageUI (`MFMessageComposeViewController`, falling back to `UIActivityViewController`), LocalAuthentication, StoreKit 2 types only insofar as needed for a static paywall — no live wiring. No AR; the spec's "AR/local ML" language is honestly interpreted as OCR only. No on-device LLM in v1.

**Persistence:** SwiftData in an App Group–shared `ModelContainer` from the very first commit — this is a resolved architecture requirement, not an optimization, because it's the only way widgets and the Siri/App Intent can read real data; the entitlement must be added to the main app, widget extension, and intent extension targets identically, and that's now an explicit build-verification checklist item since forgetting it on one target fails silently (empty-state widget forever, not a crash). `VersionedSchema`/`SchemaMigrationPlan` from schema v1, even though there's only one version today, so the first post-launch field addition isn't a destructive migration. Domain objects: `Book`, `Entry`, `Circle`, `CircleMember`, `ShareEvent` (its own object, immutable snapshot, for the outbound-only sharing model), `AppSettings`. Stable IDs everywhere, repository-style boundaries over SwiftData so a future CloudKit/private-sync adapter doesn't require a rewrite. Stats/timeline queries get an explicit CI gate — an in-memory store seeded with ~1000 entries, asserting query time stays in budget — so "fine at 20 entries, bad at 500" is caught by tests, not discovered in the field.

**Permissions/privacy:** No account, no network dependency, no push notifications, no Photos permission. Camera permission requested only at the moment OCR is invoked (`NSCameraUsageDescription`), gated by an app-owned authorization check before any camera UI — including VisionKit's — is ever presented, so the custom denied-state screen with a working manual-entry fallback is fully owned regardless of capture framework. Contacts permission is never requested at all (contact picker sidesteps the entitlement). `NSFaceIDUsageDescription` required for optional biometric app lock (off by default). `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` privacy manifest required even for a fully local app, since SwiftData/UserDefaults/file-timestamp APIs fall under Apple's "required reason" categories — skipping this risks an App Store Connect rejection on a wholly avoidable technicality.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party packages. Both agents independently and repeatedly concluded nothing in this feature set justifies license review, binary size, or update-churn risk — this trivially satisfies the phase's permissive-dependency-policy check by having no dependencies to check.

**Testing implications:** Streak/stats logic as pure functions taking an injected `Clock`/`DateProvider`, tested across midnight/timezone edge cases. OCR tested via fixtures (real photographed book pages run through `VNRecognizeTextRequest` directly), never by driving a live camera in a UI test. `DocumentScanService` and the OCR boundary both fakeable for previews and deterministic UI-flow tests. Schema migration tests from v1 onward. A real-device-only verification checklist named explicitly rather than assumed: denied camera (via Settings, not just inferred from code), `canSendText() == false` (always false in Simulator, so Simulator testing only ever proves the share-sheet fallback path), and the 20-real-book OCR accuracy spike, run early rather than late, since it's the one assumption ("OCR just works") nobody can verify by reasoning about code.

**StoreKit:** Explicitly not wired in v1 — no `.storekit` config, no partial transaction scaffolding "for later." The paywall is static UI with honest "coming soon" copy and local feature-flag gating only, both to respect the product decision that billing isn't live yet and to avoid App Store review risk from a purchase button that looks real but does nothing.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# chapterly — Ios Architecture Review

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-07 00:56:00._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-b3
Selected app slug: chapterly

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Chapterly

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Chapterly

## Category

Social Networking

## One-Sentence Promise

A personal reading journal that turns solitary reading into shared discussion with the two or three people who actually finish books with you.

## Target User

Adults who read regularly and want deeper engagement than a library app, without a public Goodreads-style feed.

## Painful Moment Solved

Finishing a book with a strong reaction and having nowhere specific to put it — group chats bury book talk, public feeds feel performative.

## Why It Is Different

Solo-first private journal and quote capture with page-synced private circles of 2-6 people invited via Contacts/Messages, not a public algorithmic feed.

## Why Users Would Pay

Unlimited circles/books, OCR quote capture from physical pages, yearly reading wrap-up exports, on-device discussion prompts.

## Subscription Model

Free: 1 circle, manual quotes. Paid monthly/annual: unlimited circles, OCR capture, advanced stats/export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

On-device journal and circle data; circle exchange for v1 happens via Messages/share-sheet delivery of entries, no server.

## Future Backend Roadmap

CloudKit/private server sync for real-time circle discussion threads.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision OCR for photographed quotes; optional on-device LLM to generate discussion questions from a synopsis.

## Design Direction

Warm, literary, editorial — serif headlines, paper textures, generous whitespace; feels like a nice notebook, not a feed.

## App Store Positioning

The private, beautiful way to talk about the books you actually finish — not another public feed.

## Invalidation Criteria

If solo retention alone doesn't hold at 4 weeks and few users ever invite a second person, rethink circles as an optional export rather than a core loop.

## v1 Build Scope

Personal reading log, manual+OCR quote capture, one circle via Messages share, personal stats.

## Core Workflows

- Log a book
- Capture a quote (manual or OCR)
- Rate and reflect
- Invite a circle via Messages
- View personal reading timeline/stats

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision OCR for quote capture
- Share Sheet/Messages extension for invites
- Widgets for currently-reading and streak
- App Intents/Siri for logging a quote

## Key Risks

- Circles need a second person to show full value; solo users may never invite anyone
- On-device OCR accuracy on varied print/lighting
- Competing with generic notes apps for the same journaling habit

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt
```
