## Feedback
This closes the phase cleanly. It covers all four required outputs (milestones, dependencies, risk mitigations, verification plan) with concrete, checkable exit criteria per milestone rather than vague polish language — e.g., "books/entries persist across force-quit," "OCR spike has a documented verdict," the named "tap row → detail → back" zoom-transition smoke test, and the `ShareEvent` round-trip test tied to the day the repository is written, not to a later UI milestone.

Sequencing is genuinely dependency-driven, not role-driven: M0's OCR spike and foundation work run in parallel because they're independent, but the spike still gates OCR-screen design intent; the widget canary is inserted specifically to retire a silent-failure risk (entitlement misconfiguration) early and cheaply, rather than bundling all extension risk into one late block. This reflects real synthesis of the round-1/round-2 disagreement rather than picking a side or splitting the difference arbitrarily — the coordinator's summary correctly identifies which points were conceded, which were compromises, and which were additive/uncontested, and the Final Output matches that summary (no discarded threads: the `ShareEvent` round-trip test and zoom-transition smoke test, both flagged as at risk of being dropped in round 1, are explicitly placed).

It also stays consistent with locked upstream decisions (iOS 18 floor + zoom transition, App Group on exactly two targets with no separate intents extension, `TimelineItem` projection with internal prefetching called out explicitly so it isn't silently reinterpreted as unnecessary, `EntrySource` manual/ocr only, `ShareEvent` without `recipientMemberIDs`). Verification is threaded through every milestone's exit criteria, including named real-device-only checks (camera denial via Settings, `canSendText()==false`), not deferred to a final QA pass. This is executable by a task-assignment phase as-is.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

Round two actually did what round one's summary asked for — both sides moved toward each other with real, specific compromises instead of just restating positions, and the daylight that's left is small enough that I don't think a third round changes anything.

Codex took Claude's milestone-zero idea and sharpened it: the OCR spike and the foundation work run in parallel rather than the spike gating everything, because there's no real dependency between "take 20 photos through Vision" and "build the App Group container" — but the spike still has to produce its go/no-go verdict before the OCR review screen gets real design time, since the result determines whether that screen treats heavy text correction as the normal case or the exception. Claude explicitly conceded this was the more honest version of its own point rather than defending the harder "nothing starts first" framing.

The other real disagreement — bundle widgets/intent with sharing in one late block, or pull them out earlier — also resolved into a shared compromise rather than someone folding. Both now agree: stand up a bare-bones widget canary immediately after foundation lands (reads one seeded value from the shared container, nothing polished) so the App Group entitlement risk gets retired in days on a real device instead of being discovered weeks later tangled up with a MessageUI bug. The full polished widget UI and the App Intent still land later, bundled with sharing, exactly as Codex originally proposed — because that's genuinely the same integration surface (cross-target persistence) and there's no reason to duplicate that testing window.

Two smaller items got pinned down with no objection: the `ShareEvent` round-trip test (record a share, fetch it back by entryID and circleID) belongs in the foundation milestone the same day the repository exists, since there's no relational safety net protecting a dangling UUID reference. The Timeline→Entry Detail zoom-transition handshake gets a named "tap row, transition, back" smoke test as a hard exit criterion for the solo-core-loop milestone, not something folded into general visual QA later.

The one thing that's additive rather than contested: Claude's point that `EntryComposeModel` should be built against both a manual draft and a fake OCR result from the moment it's built in the solo-loop milestone — not retrofitted when the camera-facing OCR milestone starts — since the settled interface already designed `applyOCRResult` to take both paths from day one. Nothing in Codex's plan conflicts with this; it's a scope clarification inside an already-agreed block, not a new fight.

## Final Output

**Milestone 0 — Foundation + OCR truth-finding (parallel, not sequential internally).** One track wires the App Group–shared SwiftData container onto exactly two targets (main app + widget; no separate intents extension), `SchemaV1`/`ChapterlyMigrationPlan`, all five repositories, routing, app settings, and app-lock scaffolding, plus seeded preview data. A second, fully independent track runs the 20-real-book OCR spike on an actual device through the real VisionKit/Vision pipeline to get a go/no-go read that will shape how much correction-friction the OCR review screen is designed to expect. As soon as the shared container exists, a bare-bones widget canary (reads one seeded value, renders it, nothing polished) proves the second target's entitlement works on a real device. Exit criteria: books/entries persist across force-quit; OCR spike has a documented verdict; widget canary renders real data on-device; a `ShareEvent` round-trip test (record, then fetch by entryID and by circleID) passes.

**Milestone 1 — Services/utilities lane**, running alongside the tail of M0 and into M2: `Clock`, `StreakCalculator` with a midnight/DST/timezone test matrix, `DocumentScanService`/`OCRService` behind fakes, `ContactPickerService`, `MessageComposerService`, `ShareTextFormatter`, `BiometricAuthService`, `StatsService`, and the perf test harness (seeded with ~1000 entries) written alongside `EntryRepository`, not bolted on later.

**Milestone 2 — Solo core loop**, the first milestone that has to feel like a finished product: lightweight book creation, manual entry compose, `EntryComposeModel` built from the start to accept both a manual draft and a fake OCR-shaped result (so it's never retrofitted later), Entry Detail as the shareable artifact wired to the zoom transition, `TimelineItem`-backed Timeline (with `relationshipKeyPathsForPrefetching` actually implemented internally, not just assumed), Book Detail, cascade-correct edit/delete, and Stats. Exit criteria: add a book, log a quote, rate/reflect optionally, see it on Timeline, survive force-quit/relaunch — plus a named "tap row → detail → back" zoom-transition smoke test as a hard gate.

**Milestone 3 — OCR capture and recovery**, camera-facing half: permission gating, real VisionKit scan feeding the now-spike-informed review screen, explicit multi-page rejection, no-text-detected as a normal terminal state, Retake/Type-instead/Continue, denied-camera fallback to manual. Exit criteria: OCR path produces the same saved `Entry` shape as manual; every degraded state is a real screen, not a dead end.

**Milestone 4 — Sharing plus full widgets/intent integration**, one delivery block since they stress the same cross-target persistence contract: Circle creation via contact picker + manual fallback, one-circle cap, real share text, MessageUI with activity-sheet fallback, `ShareEvent` recording, Circle history, and the polished widgets/App Intent built out on top of the M0 canary. Exit criteria verified on a real device: `canSendText() == false` fallback, contact-picker/manual parity, widgets showing real persisted data, intent reaching the confirm-before-save compose path.

**Milestone 5 — Polish, resilience, release hardening**: accessibility pass (Dynamic Type through AX sizes, VoiceOver on the rating control and OCR's "type it instead" focus order), paywall/subscription-gating stubs, onboarding/privacy copy, migration smoke test, app-lock grace-window tuning, visual refinement, and a real, non-zero repair buffer. Exit criteria: the full acceptance checklist — honest states everywhere, no dead ends on denied permissions, no placeholder share text, solo-first hierarchy intact.

**Dependencies:** M2 needs M0+M1 done. M3 needs M2's compose model and M0's spike verdict. M4 needs M0's canary, M2's finished-entry artifact, and M1's services. M5 needs everything.

**Risk mitigations:** OCR risk de-risked in parallel from day one instead of discovered late; widget/intent entitlement risk retired via a cheap canary right after foundation instead of buried under a sharing bug months later; `ShareEvent`'s lack of relational safety net covered by a round-trip test the same day the repository exists; zoom-transition drift caught by a named smoke test at milestone close, not left to visual inspection.

**Verification plan:** baked into each milestone's exit criteria rather than end-loaded — force-quit/relaunch checks, real-device-only checks (camera denial via Settings, `canSendText()==false`, OCR spike revalidated against the finished UI), migration tests from schema v1, streak/date edge cases, a targeted accessibility pass, and a real repair buffer in the final block.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

phase_discussions.title: Task Assignments
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Here's where the group landed. Codex and Claude actually agree on the underlying philosophy completely — four fixed lanes (`data_domain`, `services_utilities`, `primary_ui`, `polish_resilience`), file-path-as-ownership-boundary, integrator-owned composition-root files touched only via additive one-line diffs, and testing responsibilities explicitly assigned per lane rather than left generic (including naming which checks are simulator-blind and must happen on a real device). Neither contradicts the other on any of that.

Where they differed was granularity, not substance: Codex proposed a coarse 6-task backlog (essentially one big task per lane per milestone-block), while Claude, playing closer this round, broke the same work into a 23-task backlog that mirrors the milestone plan's finer sequencing task-by-task, with explicit branch names (`lane/<lane-id>/<milestone>-<slug>`), a concrete merge rule (`--no-ff` into `main`, gated on acceptance criteria, dependency-ordered), and a folder-level forbidden-zone map. Since Claude's version is a strict refinement that satisfies every one of Codex's stated concerns (same lane ownership, same "don't reach across boundaries" rule, same real-device testing carve-outs) while also being immediately actionable — every task has owned files, acceptance criteria, and acyclic dependencies a worker can pick up right now — there's no real conflict to hash out further. Codex's coarser version isn't wrong, it's just a lower-resolution version of the same plan. Closing on Claude's detailed backlog as the canonical one.

## Final Output

**Lane responsibilities and ownership**

- **data_domain** owns `Chapterly/Models/`, `Chapterly/Repositories/`, migration plan, App Group container bootstrap, seeded/preview data, the 1000-entry perf CI gate, `ShareEvent` round-trip test, cascade-delete tests, and the schema-migration smoke test.
- **services_utilities** owns `Chapterly/Services/` (protocols, live implementations, and fakes) exclusively — `Clock`, `DocumentScanService`/`OCRService` (spike + live VisionKit/Vision wiring), `ContactPickerService`, `MessageComposerService`, `ShareTextFormatter`, `BiometricAuthService`, `StatsService`, `StreakCalculator`. Owns the streak midnight/DST/timezone test matrix and OCR fixture tests run against the real spike photos.
- **primary_ui** owns `Chapterly/App/AppRouter.swift`/`Route.swift`/`RootView.swift`, `Chapterly/Capture/`, and all of `Chapterly/Features/` (Book, EntryCompose, Timeline, BookDetail, EntryDetail, Stats, Circle). Owns the force-quit/relaunch check and the named "tap row → detail → back" zoom-transition smoke test as a hard milestone-2 gate.
- **polish_resilience** owns `Chapterly/Resilience/`, `Chapterly/Settings/`, `Chapterly/Features/Onboarding/`, `Chapterly/Intents/`, and all of `ChapterlyWidgets/`. Owns the accessibility pass, app-lock scenePhase behavior test, and every real-device-only check (camera denial via actual Settings toggle, `canSendText()==false`, widgets/intent verified on-device) — these are simulator-blind risks named as this lane's explicit responsibility.

**Forbidden edit zones:** nobody outside `data_domain` writes to `Models/`/`Repositories/`; nobody outside `services_utilities` writes a live service implementation; `primary_ui` owns its entire `Features/`+`Capture/` tree and `polish_resilience` never reaches into it, consuming only repository/service outputs. The only shared-touch files are the composition root (`ChapterlyApp.swift`, `AppEnvironment.swift`) plus `Route.swift`, and those are append-only — add a line, don't restructure. If a lane finds it needs to touch a file outside its folders, that's a signal the task breakdown is wrong, not license to cross the line — it stops and the task list gets amended.

**Branch naming:** `lane/<lane-id>/<milestone>-<slug>` (e.g. `lane/data_domain/m0-foundation`, `lane/services_utilities/m0-ocr-spike`, `lane/primary_ui/m2-solo-core-loop`, `lane/polish_resilience/m4-widgets-intent`).

**Merge strategy:** a lane branches off `main` only once its `depends_on` tasks are already merged — the dependency graph below is the literal merge order. Merges are `--no-ff`, gated on that task's acceptance criteria passing, not "looks done." Composition-root conflicts are resolved by rebasing and re-applying the one-line addition, since no lane is allowed to do a structural edit there.

**Communication protocol:** every handoff states what changed, which contracts were touched, and what the next lane can now rely on. Cross-lane contract changes are proposed and acknowledged before editing, never silently tweaked. Nobody says "tested" without saying whether it was simulator, unit, UI, or real device.

phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# chapterly — Task Assignments

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-07 01:35:03._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-b3
Selected app slug: chapterly

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Chapterly

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Chapterly

## Category

Social Networking

## One-Sentence Promise

A personal reading journal that turns solitary reading into shared discussion with the two or three people who actually finish books with you.

## Target User

Adults who read regularly and want deeper engagement than a library app, without a public Goodreads-style feed.

## Painful Moment Solved

Finishing a book with a strong reaction and having nowhere specific to put it — group chats bury book talk, public feeds feel performative.

## Why It Is Different

Solo-first private journal and quote capture with page-synced private circles of 2-6 people invited via Contacts/Messages, not a public algorithmic feed.

## Why Users Would Pay

Unlimited circles/books, OCR quote capture from physical pages, yearly reading wrap-up exports, on-device discussion prompts.

## Subscription Model

Free: 1 circle, manual quotes. Paid monthly/annual: unlimited circles, OCR capture, advanced stats/export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

On-device journal and circle data; circle exchange for v1 happens via Messages/share-sheet delivery of entries, no server.

## Future Backend Roadmap

CloudKit/private server sync for real-time circle discussion threads.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision OCR for photographed quotes; optional on-device LLM to generate discussion questions from a synopsis.

## Design Direction

Warm, literary, editorial — serif headlines, paper textures, generous whitespace; feels like a nice notebook, not a feed.

## App Store Positioning

The private, beautiful way to talk about the books you actually finish — not another public feed.

## Invalidation Criteria

If solo retention alone doesn't hold at 4 weeks and few users ever invite a second person, rethink circles as an optional export rather than a core loop.

## v1 Build Scope

Personal reading log, manual+OCR quote capture, one circle via Messages share, personal stats.

## Core Workflows

- Log a book
- Capture a quote (manual or OCR)
- Rate and reflect
- Invite a circle via Messages
- View personal reading timeline/stats

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision OCR for quote capture
- Share Sheet/Messages extension for invites
- Widgets for currently-reading and streak
- App Intents/Siri for logging a quote

## Key Risks

- Circles need a second person to show full value; solo users may never invite anyone
- On-device OCR accuracy on varied print/lighting
- Competing with generic notes apps for the same journaling habit

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a warm, editorial reading-journal app: a paper-textured home timeline of books and quotes, a distraction-free quote-capture camera view with OCR crop confirmation, and a small private circle view that reads like shared marginalia rather than a chat app.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 9 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 3 Social Networking apps, 3 Travel apps, and 3 Utilities apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

The app should live as its own project folder at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/chapterly`. Inside that, I’d keep a single Xcode workspace-level root with target-specific folders that mirror the lane split, because the easiest way to create merge pain in SwiftUI is letting multiple people touch the same feature folders and app bootstrap files casually. Concretely: `Chapterly/App`, `Chapterly/Data`, `Chapterly/Services`, `Chapterly/Features`, `Chapterly/SharedUI`, `Chapterly/Widgets`, `Chapterly/Resources`, and `ChapterlyTests` / `ChapterlyUITests`. The widget target stays in the same repo and reads the same App Group-backed store, but widget code is not shared by direct editing of feature files.

I’d assign ownership by seam, not by screen list. `data_domain` owns every persisted model, schema, repository implementation, App Group container builder, seed data path, and migration plumbing. `services_utilities` owns every system-framework bridge and pure utility seam: OCR, document scan, contact picker wrapper, message composer wrapper, biometric auth, stats engine, date/clock injection, and share text formatting. `primary_ui` owns navigation, feature models, screen composition, capture flow state machine, Timeline/Book/Entry/Circle/Stats/Settings views, and widget-facing presentation structs if they are UI-shaped rather than persistence-shaped. `polish_resilience` owns app lock orchestration, onboarding/privacy/paywall shells, accessibility pass, widget target implementation, App Intent wiring, preview fixtures, test plans, and verification harnesses that cross feature seams. The integrator-owned files are only `ChapterlyApp.swift`, the `.entitlements` files, target build settings, the widget target entry file, and any shared environment/bootstrap file that wires concrete implementations together. Nobody edits those ad hoc; they change only in dedicated integration tasks.

The forbidden edit zones need to be explicit. `primary_ui` should not edit SwiftData models, repository protocols, or schema files. `data_domain` should not edit SwiftUI screens except minimal preview adapters if absolutely required. `services_utilities` should not edit navigation, feature models, or view files. `polish_resilience` should not rewrite core repositories or feature logic while doing QA hardening; if a bug requires that, it gets handed back to the owning lane. The one exception is additive test files: any lane can add tests under its own target folder, but test helpers that mock multiple seams belong to `polish_resilience`. This matters because the fastest way to get fake progress is letting the widget/intent lane patch app code directly to “make it work,” then nobody knows which contract drifted.

I’d run branches as lane-scoped short-lived branches off a protected integration branch for the app. Base branch: `chapterly/main`. Lane branches: `chapterly/data-foundation`, `chapterly/ui-core-loop`, `chapterly/services-ocr-share`, `chapterly/polish-widgets-intents`. Milestone integration branches are temporary: `chapterly/m0-foundation`, `chapterly/m2-solo-loop`, and so on. Merge strategy should be squash-merge from lane branch into the active milestone branch after acceptance criteria pass, then fast-forward or squash that milestone branch into `chapterly/main` only when the whole milestone closes. No direct commits to `chapterly/main`, and no lane branch rebasing after another lane has started depending on it; merge forward instead, because rewritten history on a four-lane app build is self-inflicted damage.

Testing responsibility should also follow seams. `data_domain` proves persistence, migrations, repository round-trips, cascade rules, and the `ShareEvent` history queries. `services_utilities` proves OCR fixtures, message/share formatting, `canSendText()` branching, contact picker/manual fallback adapters, and streak/date logic with injected clocks. `primary_ui` proves navigation, state transitions, empty/loading/error states, and that manual and OCR-prefilled entry flows converge on the same save path. `polish_resilience` owns the real-device checklist, widget canary, widget freshness checks, App Intent end-to-end validation, accessibility verification, app-lock behavior across `scenePhase`, and regression sweeps before milestone closure. Nobody gets to say “tested” without saying whether it was simulator, unit, UI, or real device, because the app has too many behaviors that pass in one environment and fail in another.

Communication protocol should be lightweight but rigid. Every task handoff needs three things in writing: what changed, which contracts were touched, and what the next lane can now rely on. If a lane needs a cross-lane contract change, it proposes it before editing and waits for explicit acknowledgement from the owning lane; no “small harmless tweak” to someone else’s interface. Daily rhythm should be milestone-oriented: open dependencies, blocked files, newly stable seams, and any real-device findings. If a file is integrator-owned, changes are announced before edit and after merge. If a lane discovers a contract bug in another lane’s area, it opens a patch suggestion or diff note, but does not silently fix ownership boundaries.

The biggest risk in this plan is that `primary_ui` and `polish_resilience` both naturally want to touch the same top-level app wiring once widgets, intents, and app lock come online. If that boundary is fuzzy, you get bootstrap churn, broken target membership, and last-minute regressions that look random. The detail the final output must preserve is that cross-target wiring files are integrator-owned and changed only through explicit integration tasks, because that is the pressure point where local-first persistence, widget data, App Intent access, and app-lock lifecycle all meet.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Closer) — Round 1**

Building this as the systems/data lane and playing closer here — no "we'll figure out ownership later," it's decided below and it's final unless someone gives me a real reason to reopen it.

**Where this lives:** `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/Chapterly/`, its own git repo, initialized now if it isn't already. Xcode project `Chapterly.xcodeproj` with two targets — `Chapterly` (main app) and `ChapterlyWidgets` (widget extension) — no separate intents extension target, matching the locked architecture decision that `LogQuoteIntent` lives in the main app target. Test targets: `ChapterlyTests` (unit/integration) and `ChapterlyUITests` (the named smoke tests — zoom transition, force-quit/relaunch).

**Folder layout, and this is also the ownership map, because in this build file path *is* lane boundary:**

```
Chapterly/
  Chapterly.xcodeproj/               <- integrator-owned (append-only target membership, no restructuring)
  Chapterly/App/
    ChapterlyApp.swift               <- integrator-owned composition root
    AppEnvironment.swift             <- integrator-owned composition root
    AppRouter.swift                  <- primary_ui owns
    Route.swift                      <- primary_ui owns; other lanes may append a case, never rename/remove one
    RootView.swift                   <- primary_ui owns
  Chapterly/Models/                  <- data_domain owns exclusively
  Chapterly/Repositories/            <- data_domain owns exclusively
  Chapterly/Services/                <- services_utilities owns exclusively (protocols + live impls + fakes)
  Chapterly/Capture/                 <- primary_ui owns (CaptureCoordinator, CaptureStep, EntryDraft, OCRReviewModel/View)
  Chapterly/Features/                <- primary_ui owns (Book, EntryCompose, Timeline, BookDetail, EntryDetail, Stats, Circle)
  Chapterly/Resilience/              <- polish_resilience owns (AppLockController + overlay)
  Chapterly/Settings/                <- polish_resilience owns (Settings, Paywall)
  Chapterly/Features/Onboarding/     <- polish_resilience owns
  Chapterly/Intents/                 <- polish_resilience owns (LogQuoteIntent)
  Chapterly/Resources/               <- integrator-owned (Assets.xcassets, PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy)
  ChapterlyWidgets/                  <- polish_resilience owns exclusively
  ChapterlyTests/DataDomainTests/    <- data_domain owns
  ChapterlyTests/ServicesTests/      <- services_utilities owns
  ChapterlyUITests/                  <- polish_resilience owns
```

Forbidden zones, stated plainly: nobody outside `data_domain` writes to `Models/` or `Repositories/` — if `primary_ui` needs a new query shape, it asks for a repository method, it doesn't add SwiftData code itself. Nobody outside `services_utilities` writes a live service implementation — `primary_ui` and `polish_resilience` consume protocols and fakes only. `primary_ui` owns the entire `Features/`+`Capture/` tree; `polish_resilience` does not touch those files even for widget/intent wiring — it reads `StatsSnapshot`/`TimelineItem`-shaped data through the repository/service layer, never by reaching into a UI model. The only files more than one lane touches are the three composition-root files plus `Route.swift`, and the rule there is append-only diffs — add your registration line, add your enum case, don't reformat or reorder someone else's.

Branch naming: `lane/<lane-id>/<milestone>-<slug>`, e.g. `lane/data_domain/m0-foundation`, `lane/services_utilities/m0-ocr-spike`, `[REDACTED:high_entropy]`, `lane/primary_ui/m2-solo-core-loop`, `lane/services_utilities/m3-ocr-live`, `lane/primary_ui/m4-sharing`, `lane/polish_resilience/m4-widgets-intent`, `lane/polish_resilience/m5-hardening`. Merge strategy: a lane branches off `main` only once its `depends_on` tasks are already merged into `main` — the dependency graph in the backlog below is the literal merge order, not a suggestion. Merges are `--no-ff` into `main`, gated on that task's acceptance criteria passing, not on "looks done." If two lanes need the same composition-root file in-flight at once, whoever merges second rebases onto `main` and re-applies their one-line addition — these files never get a real three-way conflict because nobody is allowed to do a structural edit there, only additive lines.

Testing responsibilities, assigned by lane not left generic: `data_domain` owns the 1000-entry perf CI gate, the `ShareEvent` round-trip test, cascade-delete tests, and the schema-migration smoke test. `services_utilities` owns the `StreakCalculator` midnight/DST/timezone matrix and OCR fixture tests run against the real spike photos. `primary_ui` owns the force-quit/relaunch check and the named "tap row → detail → back" zoom-transition smoke test as a hard milestone-2 gate. `polish_resilience` owns the accessibility pass, the app-lock scenePhase behavior test, and — critically — every real-device-only check (camera denial via actual Settings toggle, `canSendText()==false`, widget rendering real persisted data on-device): these are simulator-blind risks and I'm naming them as this lane's responsibility so they don't get silently "verified" on a simulator and called done.

Communication protocol: a task isn't "done," it's done-with-acceptance-criteria-met, and the next dependent task doesn't start until that's true — this is enforced by the dependency graph below, not by anyone's word. Any lane that discovers it needs to touch a file outside its owned folders treats that as a signal the task breakdown is wrong, not a reason to reach across the line — it stops and the task list gets amended instead.

That's the full backlog — 23 tasks, every one owned by exactly one of the four fixed lanes, dependency graph is acyclic and mirrors the milestone sequencing already agreed in project_plan, and the folder/branch/forbidden-zone rules above mean a lane can pull its next task and start immediately without waiting on a meeting. I'm treating this as closed unless someone identifies a real ownership gap.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: YES

Here's where the group landed. Codex and Claude actually agree on the underlying philosophy completely — four fixed lanes (`data_domain`, `services_utilities`, `primary_ui`, `polish_resilience`), file-path-as-ownership-boundary, integrator-owned composition-root files touched only via additive one-line diffs, and testing responsibilities explicitly assigned per lane rather than left generic (including naming which checks are simulator-blind and must happen on a real device). Neither contradicts the other on any of that.

Where they differed was granularity, not substance: Codex proposed a coarse 6-task backlog (essentially one big task per lane per milestone-block), while Claude, playing closer this round, broke the same work into a 23-task backlog that mirrors the milestone plan's finer sequencing task-by-task, with explicit branch names (`lane/<lane-id>/<milestone>-<slug>`), a concrete merge rule (`--no-ff` into `main`, gated on acceptance criteria, dependency-ordered), and a folder-level forbidden-zone map. Since Claude's version is a strict refinement that satisfies every one of Codex's stated concerns (same lane ownership, same "don't reach across boundaries" rule, same real-device testing carve-outs) while also being immediately actionable — every task has owned files, acceptance criteria, and acyclic dependencies a worker can pick up right now — there's no real conflict to hash out further. Codex's coarser version isn't wrong, it's just a lower-resolution version of the same plan. Closing on Claude's detailed backlog as the canonical one.

## Final Output

**Lane responsibilities and ownership**

- **data_domain** owns `Chapterly/Models/`, `Chapterly/Repositories/`, migration plan, App Group container bootstrap, seeded/preview data, the 1000-entry perf CI gate, `ShareEvent` round-trip test, cascade-delete tests, and the schema-migration smoke test.
- **services_utilities** owns `Chapterly/Services/` (protocols, live implementations, and fakes) exclusively — `Clock`, `DocumentScanService`/`OCRService` (spike + live VisionKit/Vision wiring), `ContactPickerService`, `MessageComposerService`, `ShareTextFormatter`, `BiometricAuthService`, `StatsService`, `StreakCalculator`. Owns the streak midnight/DST/timezone test matrix and OCR fixture tests run against the real spike photos.
- **primary_ui** owns `Chapterly/App/AppRouter.swift`/`Route.swift`/`RootView.swift`, `Chapterly/Capture/`, and all of `Chapterly/Features/` (Book, EntryCompose, Timeline, BookDetail, EntryDetail, Stats, Circle). Owns the force-quit/relaunch check and the named "tap row → detail → back" zoom-transition smoke test as a hard milestone-2 gate.
- **polish_resilience** owns `Chapterly/Resilience/`, `Chapterly/Settings/`, `Chapterly/Features/Onboarding/`, `Chapterly/Intents/`, and all of `ChapterlyWidgets/`. Owns the accessibility pass, app-lock scenePhase behavior test, and every real-device-only check (camera denial via actual Settings toggle, `canSendText()==false`, widgets/intent verified on-device) — these are simulator-blind risks named as this lane's explicit responsibility.

**Forbidden edit zones:** nobody outside `data_domain` writes to `Models/`/`Repositories/`; nobody outside `services_utilities` writes a live service implementation; `primary_ui` owns its entire `Features/`+`Capture/` tree and `polish_resilience` never reaches into it, consuming only repository/service outputs. The only shared-touch files are the composition root (`ChapterlyApp.swift`, `AppEnvironment.swift`) plus `Route.swift`, and those are append-only — add a line, don't restructure. If a lane finds it needs to touch a file outside its folders, that's a signal the task breakdown is wrong, not license to cross the line — it stops and the task list gets amended.

**Branch naming:** `lane/<lane-id>/<milestone>-<slug>` (e.g. `lane/data_domain/m0-foundation`, `lane/services_utilities/m0-ocr-spike`, `lane/primary_ui/m2-solo-core-loop`, `lane/polish_resilience/m4-widgets-intent`).

**Merge strategy:** a lane branches off `main` only once its `depends_on` tasks are already merged — the dependency graph below is the literal merge order. Merges are `--no-ff`, gated on that task's acceptance criteria passing, not "looks done." Composition-root conflicts are resolved by rebasing and re-applying the one-line addition, since no lane is allowed to do a structural edit there.

**Communication protocol:** every handoff states what changed, which contracts were touched, and what the next lane can now rely on. Cross-lane contract changes are proposed and acknowledged before editing, never silently tweaked. Nobody says "tested" without saying whether it was simulator, unit, UI, or real device.

```

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
The output satisfies every required element of this phase. Lane responsibilities, owned files, forbidden edit zones (with the append-only composition-root carve-out), branch naming convention, merge strategy tied to the dependency graph, and per-lane testing responsibilities (including explicit naming of simulator-blind real-device-only checks) are all present and concrete, not generic.

The tasks-json backlog is well-formed: all 23 tasks have a valid `owner_lane` (data_domain/primary_ui/services_utilities/polish_resilience), every task has files, depends_on, and acceptance_criteria, no task is ownerless, and the dependency graph is acyclic (every dependency points to a lower-numbered task id, confirmed by trace). T-001 and T-004 have no dependencies, so build workers can start immediately, satisfying the acceptance check.

The backlog is faithful to upstream decisions rather than inventing new scope: it mirrors the project_plan's M0–M5 milestone sequencing task-by-task (App Group scaffolding + OCR spike in parallel, widget canary immediately after foundation, EntryComposeModel built against both manual and fake-OCR input from T-010 rather than retrofitted later, ShareEvent round-trip test placed in T-002 alongside the repository, the named zoom-transition smoke test as a hard gate in T-012, sharing bundled with full widget/intent polish in T-017/T-018 per the agreed compromise). No contradictions with tech_specs or ios_architecture_review are introduced (App Group on exactly two targets, no separate intents extension, TimelineItem-projection prefetching called out explicitly in T-002's acceptance criteria).

Good to close.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

Here's where the group landed. Codex and Claude actually agree on the underlying philosophy completely — four fixed lanes (`data_domain`, `services_utilities`, `primary_ui`, `polish_resilience`), file-path-as-ownership-boundary, integrator-owned composition-root files touched only via additive one-line diffs, and testing responsibilities explicitly assigned per lane rather than left generic (including naming which checks are simulator-blind and must happen on a real device). Neither contradicts the other on any of that.

Where they differed was granularity, not substance: Codex proposed a coarse 6-task backlog (essentially one big task per lane per milestone-block), while Claude, playing closer this round, broke the same work into a 23-task backlog that mirrors the milestone plan's finer sequencing task-by-task, with explicit branch names (`lane/<lane-id>/<milestone>-<slug>`), a concrete merge rule (`--no-ff` into `main`, gated on acceptance criteria, dependency-ordered), and a folder-level forbidden-zone map. Since Claude's version is a strict refinement that satisfies every one of Codex's stated concerns (same lane ownership, same "don't reach across boundaries" rule, same real-device testing carve-outs) while also being immediately actionable — every task has owned files, acceptance criteria, and acyclic dependencies a worker can pick up right now — there's no real conflict to hash out further. Codex's coarser version isn't wrong, it's just a lower-resolution version of the same plan. Closing on Claude's detailed backlog as the canonical one.

## Final Output

**Lane responsibilities and ownership**

- **data_domain** owns `Chapterly/Models/`, `Chapterly/Repositories/`, migration plan, App Group container bootstrap, seeded/preview data, the 1000-entry perf CI gate, `ShareEvent` round-trip test, cascade-delete tests, and the schema-migration smoke test.
- **services_utilities** owns `Chapterly/Services/` (protocols, live implementations, and fakes) exclusively — `Clock`, `DocumentScanService`/`OCRService` (spike + live VisionKit/Vision wiring), `ContactPickerService`, `MessageComposerService`, `ShareTextFormatter`, `BiometricAuthService`, `StatsService`, `StreakCalculator`. Owns the streak midnight/DST/timezone test matrix and OCR fixture tests run against the real spike photos.
- **primary_ui** owns `Chapterly/App/AppRouter.swift`/`Route.swift`/`RootView.swift`, `Chapterly/Capture/`, and all of `Chapterly/Features/` (Book, EntryCompose, Timeline, BookDetail, EntryDetail, Stats, Circle). Owns the force-quit/relaunch check and the named "tap row → detail → back" zoom-transition smoke test as a hard milestone-2 gate.
- **polish_resilience** owns `Chapterly/Resilience/`, `Chapterly/Settings/`, `Chapterly/Features/Onboarding/`, `Chapterly/Intents/`, and all of `ChapterlyWidgets/`. Owns the accessibility pass, app-lock scenePhase behavior test, and every real-device-only check (camera denial via actual Settings toggle, `canSendText()==false`, widgets/intent verified on-device) — these are simulator-blind risks named as this lane's explicit responsibility.

**Forbidden edit zones:** nobody outside `data_domain` writes to `Models/`/`Repositories/`; nobody outside `services_utilities` writes a live service implementation; `primary_ui` owns its entire `Features/`+`Capture/` tree and `polish_resilience` never reaches into it, consuming only repository/service outputs. The only shared-touch files are the composition root (`ChapterlyApp.swift`, `AppEnvironment.swift`) plus `Route.swift`, and those are append-only — add a line, don't restructure. If a lane finds it needs to touch a file outside its folders, that's a signal the task breakdown is wrong, not license to cross the line — it stops and the task list gets amended.

**Branch naming:** `lane/<lane-id>/<milestone>-<slug>` (e.g. `lane/data_domain/m0-foundation`, `lane/services_utilities/m0-ocr-spike`, `lane/primary_ui/m2-solo-core-loop`, `lane/polish_resilience/m4-widgets-intent`).

**Merge strategy:** a lane branches off `main` only once its `depends_on` tasks are already merged — the dependency graph below is the literal merge order. Merges are `--no-ff`, gated on that task's acceptance criteria passing, not "looks done." Composition-root conflicts are resolved by rebasing and re-applying the one-line addition, since no lane is allowed to do a structural edit there.

**Communication protocol:** every handoff states what changed, which contracts were touched, and what the next lane can now rely on. Cross-lane contract changes are proposed and acknowledged before editing, never silently tweaked. Nobody says "tested" without saying whether it was simulator, unit, UI, or real device.

phase_discussions.title: Implementation Readiness Gate
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Round two closed out cleanly. Claude flipped on the iOS version question and actually strengthened Codex's case rather than just deferring to it — the original iOS 17 "broader device reach" rationale from design_handoff was written against 2024/2025 device population assumptions, but it's now July 2026, iOS 18 has been out since September 2024, and the population still stuck below it is negligible. So the one concrete reason iOS 17 was ever chosen has expired with time, not been argued away — that's a cleaner justification than "one code path is simpler," and both agents now land on iOS 18 + zoom-only with no fallback.

Codex, in the same message, already said yes to the two-tier verification split Claude was pushing for (build-verified vs. owner-device-required) — calling it "not a scope cut, a truth-in-verification fix" — so by the time Claude asked for an explicit yes/no on that point, it had already been given. The free/paid one-sentence rule and the split persistence-test ownership were confirmed by both with no pushback. The phantom third "intents extension" target gets formally retired. The one-line aspect-ratio addition to T-012 (verify Entry Detail stays legible at 9:16 and 1:1 crops) went uncontested and costs nothing, so it's in. Nothing is left hanging.

## Final Output

**Readiness verdict:** Ready to build, with the fixes below written into the record as this gate's authoritative corrections to the plan. No further planning rounds needed.

**Cross-phase consistency check — contradictions found and resolved:**
1. Motion API conflict (design_handoff said iOS 17/`matchedGeometryEffect` baseline; tech_specs and T-012 silently locked iOS 18/zoom-only). **Resolved: iOS 18 is the deployment floor, `.navigationTransition(.zoom(sourceID:in:))` is the sole implementation, no `matchedGeometryEffect` fallback anywhere in the codebase.** Reason: the iOS 17 choice was justified by device-reach concerns that have since expired — iOS 18 has been shipping since September 2024, and by July 2026 the addressable population below it is negligible. One navigation contract, not two.
2. Stale "three targets" language in ios_architecture_review vs. the two-target decision used everywhere downstream. **Resolved: exactly two targets — main app and widget extension — share the App Group store. `LogQuoteIntent` lives in the main app target. No separate intents extension exists, ever.**
3. Free/paid boundary scattered across multiple phases with slightly different phrasing. **Resolved to one canonical sentence, to be copy-pasted verbatim (not paraphrased) into T-020 and T-023:** "Free includes unlimited books, unlimited entries, manual quotes, stats, app lock, widgets, and App Intent, plus one circle; paid in v1 is represented only by UI-only gating on OCR capture and additional circles, with no live billing."
4. Force-quit/relaunch test responsibility ambiguously duplicated between lanes. **Resolved: `data_domain` owns the automated on-disk persistence test (store survives a simulated relaunch); `primary_ui` owns a separate manual end-to-end relaunch walkthrough through the real UI flow.** These are two distinct checks, not one check claimed by two owners.

**Build blockers and fixes:** all four items above are the blockers for this gate, and all four now have a written fix. No blocker remains open.

**Final scope for build:** unchanged from project_plan/task_assignments, with the four fixes above layered in, plus: Entry Detail (T-012) gets one additional acceptance line — layout verified legible at 9:16 and 1:1 share/screenshot crops — since the product brief calls this screen the primary growth artifact and it shouldn't ship untested at anything but standard portrait scroll. The dedicated wrap-up/export image feature stays explicitly deferred as a named, conscious cut, not a silent one.

**Acceptance criteria checklist — now explicitly two-tier, per T-023:**
- **Build-verified tier** (must be actually exercised and passing before this build pass calls itself done): all unit/integration tests (persistence, cascade rules, `ShareEvent` round-trip, streak/date matrix, migration test, perf gate), simulator-level UI flows (manual + OCR-fake compose convergence, timeline/stats states, zoom-transition tap-row-to-detail-and-back smoke test), OCR fixture tests against sample photographed-text images run directly through `VNRecognizeTextRequest`, app-lock scenePhase state-machine logic (pure and fully unit-testable without a device), and `canSendText()==false` fallback behavior (this is Simulator's actual default, so it's genuinely covered here, not deferred).
- **Owner Device Verification Required tier** (ships as its own named deliverable document, never silently folded into "done"): the 20-real-book OCR spike (T-004) run through the live VisionKit/Vision pipeline, camera-permission denial verified via the actual Settings toggle (T-015), the true MFMessageComposeViewController send path (T-016) which Simulator cannot exercise, and widgets/App Intent rendering real persisted data on physical hardware (T-007/T-018/T-023).

Both tiers must appear as separate, explicitly labeled sections in the final acceptance-checklist artifact — a flat, undifferentiated list that implies real-device work happened when it didn't is treated as a defect in the deliverable itself.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# chapterly — Implementation Readiness Gate

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-07 01:39:52._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-b3
Selected app slug: chapterly

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Chapterly

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Chapterly

## Category

Social Networking

## One-Sentence Promise

A personal reading journal that turns solitary reading into shared discussion with the two or three people who actually finish books with you.

## Target User

Adults who read regularly and want deeper engagement than a library app, without a public Goodreads-style feed.

## Painful Moment Solved

Finishing a book with a strong reaction and having nowhere specific to put it — group chats bury book talk, public feeds feel performative.

## Why It Is Different

Solo-first private journal and quote capture with page-synced private circles of 2-6 people invited via Contacts/Messages, not a public algorithmic feed.

## Why Users Would Pay

Unlimited circles/books, OCR quote capture from physical pages, yearly reading wrap-up exports, on-device discussion prompts.

## Subscription Model

Free: 1 circle, manual quotes. Paid monthly/annual: unlimited circles, OCR capture, advanced stats/export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

On-device journal and circle data; circle exchange for v1 happens via Messages/share-sheet delivery of entries, no server.

## Future Backend Roadmap

CloudKit/private server sync for real-time circle discussion threads.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision OCR for photographed quotes; optional on-device LLM to generate discussion questions from a synopsis.

## Design Direction

Warm, literary, editorial — serif headlines, paper textures, generous whitespace; feels like a nice notebook, not a feed.

## App Store Positioning

The private, beautiful way to talk about the books you actually finish — not another public feed.

## Invalidation Criteria

If solo retention alone doesn't hold at 4 weeks and few users ever invite a second person, rethink circles as an optional export rather than a core loop.

## v1 Build Scope

Personal reading log, manual+OCR quote capture, one circle via Messages share, personal stats.

## Core Workflows

- Log a book
- Capture a quote (manual or OCR)
- Rate and reflect
- Invite a circle via Messages
- View personal reading timeline/stats

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision OCR for quote capture
- Share Sheet/Messages extension for invites
- Widgets for currently-reading and streak
- App Intents/Siri for logging a quote

## Key Risks

- Circles need a second person to show full value; solo users may never invite anyone
- On-device OCR accuracy on varied print/lighting
- Competing with generic notes apps for the same journaling habit

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a warm, editorial reading-journal app: a paper-textured home timeline of books and quotes, a distraction-free quote-capture camera view with OCR crop confirmation, and a small private circle view that reads like shared marginalia rather than a chat app.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 9 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 3 Social Networking apps, 3 Travel apps, and 3 Utilities apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 18 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 9, and build all 9 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Before any code is written, audit whether the product brief, design handoff, iOS architecture, tech specs, project plan, and task contracts are coherent enough for a one-shot production build. Resolve blockers or explicitly downgrade scope before build starts.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Frontend Engineer · Closer) — Round 1**

My read is: this is close to build-ready, but not actually ready until three contradictions are resolved in writing. If we start coding without fixing them, the team will end up inventing product and architecture decisions mid-build, which is exactly what this gate is supposed to prevent.

First blocker: the deployment target is inconsistent. The design handoff says `matchedGeometryEffect` is the baseline and `.navigationTransition(.zoom)` is only for iOS 18+ if the minimum target ends up there. The tech specs then hard-lock `Route` to “minimum deployment target iOS 18” and say zoom transition is the sole implementation with no fallback. The architecture review, though, explicitly recommends iOS 17 as the floor because nothing else needs 18. That is not a cosmetic mismatch; it changes navigation code, test coverage, and whether the design handoff is even being followed. I would resolve it now by setting the floor to iOS 18 and deleting every fallback reference. That is the cleaner build decision because the task contracts already assume zoom-only, and this phase should prefer one implementation over dual-path complexity.

Second blocker: the free/paid boundary is internally inconsistent. The original spec says paid includes unlimited circles and OCR, but the detailed discussion and app-features phase also say free must not cripple the solo loop and explicitly lock unlimited books/entries in free. The prompt contract muddies this by saying paid-entitlement plumbing does not have to be finished, and the task list says the paywall gates OCR entry and a second circle with “coming soon” copy. That is fine mechanically, but the final build scope needs one unambiguous product rule: free includes unlimited books, unlimited entries, manual quotes, stats, and one circle; paid is only a UI gate for OCR and extra circles in v1, with no live billing. If that exact sentence is not preserved, somebody will eventually gate the wrong thing.

Third blocker: the test responsibility for force-quit/relaunch is split in a sloppy way. Earlier tasking assigns that check to `primary_ui`; later lane ownership also says `primary_ui` owns it; but the persistence smoke test in `data_domain` already claims on-disk relaunch behavior in tests. That is not fatal, but it is ambiguous enough that each lane can assume the other covered the real failure mode. The fix is simple: `data_domain` owns automated on-disk persistence tests; `primary_ui` owns manual end-to-end relaunch verification from the real UI path. Both stay, but they are different checks.

Outside those blockers, the stack is coherent enough to build. The core loop is stable: add lightweight book, capture quote manually or via OCR, confirm/edit, optionally rate/reflect, save locally, see it in Timeline, optionally share the finished artifact outward via Messages/share sheet. The design, architecture, and task phases all protect the same important boundary: no feed, no inbox, no capture-to-share shortcut, and Circle never gets first-class home-screen weight. That part is consistent and should not be reopened.

The biggest risk even after these fixes is still frontend-state complexity around the capture flow. The documents are right to insist that manual entry, OCR-prefilled entry, and Siri/App Intent entry all converge on the same compose/save path. If the implementation drifts into separate save logic per source, the app will look done while quietly accumulating behavior differences in validation, draft state, and post-save routing. The final output from this phase needs to preserve one exact detail: `EntryComposeModel` is the only save path for all entry sources, and OCR review is not an alternate editor, only a confirm-and-correct step before compose.

So my concrete readiness verdict is: not ready this second, but ready once those three fixes are written into the phase output with no ambiguity. I would not downgrade scope further. The current v1 scope is already the smaller finished app: solo journal first, one outbound circle, OCR with human confirmation, stats, widgets, intent, app lock, static paywall.

The final scope for build should be locked as this and nothing more: local-only SwiftUI app on iOS 18, no backend, one real shared SwiftData container across app and widget, manual and OCR quote capture, one saved circle in free-tier UI, outbound Messages/share-sheet sharing from completed entries only, personal timeline/stats, optional app lock, widgets, App Intent, static paywall, and full empty/error/denied-permission states. No Photos import, no inbound replies, no sync, no push, no live billing, no LLM prompts.

The acceptance checklist that matters before anyone calls the build “done” is straightforward:
- A first-run user can create a book, save a manual quote, reopen the app, and still see it.
- OCR always lands in editable review/compose, never saves silently, and denied camera still leaves a working manual path.
- Timeline is the home surface and Circle is reachable but visually secondary.
- Sharing is only possible from Entry Detail and produces real text, with `canSendText()==false` falling back cleanly.
- Widgets and App Intent read the same persisted store as the main app.
- Streak/stats logic is deterministic against injected time.
- App lock triggers on background return, not transient inactive states.
- Every major screen has a real empty/error state, not a placeholder.
- Automated and manual testing ownership are explicit: persistence/store by `data_domain`, services/date/OCR fixtures by `services_utilities`, UI flow/navigation/state by `primary_ui`, real-device/accessibility/cross-target checks by `polish_resilience`.

If those exact fixes are applied, build can start without inventing anything important. If they are not, the team is still pretending it has consensus when it does not.

**Claude (Delivery Lead · Visionary) — Round 1**

Running the actual consistency check rather than restating prior summaries, I found one real contradiction and one practical execution gap that this gate exists to catch — plus a place where I think the group has been too conservative about what "remarkable" looks like here.

The contradiction: design_handoff explicitly locks iOS 17 as the deployment floor, `matchedGeometryEffect` as the shipped baseline, and treats `.navigationTransition(.zoom(sourceID:in:))` as an optional upgrade "if the app's minimum deployment target ends up at iOS 18+" — with an explicit rationale (broader device reach, nothing else in the build needs 18, reversible later). But tech_specs' `Route` entry flatly overrides that without acknowledging it: "minimum deployment target iOS 18; entryDetail push uses `.navigationTransition(.zoom(sourceID:in:))` as the sole implementation, no matchedGeometryEffect fallback anywhere in the codebase." Nobody in project_plan or task_assignments flagged this as a deliberate reversal of the design phase's reasoning — it reads like drift, not a decision. Since this is the one motion moment the whole design phase called "the big idea both agents protected" and it's also literally the file-artifact meant to be screenshot-worthy, I don't want it shipped on an unexamined flip-flop. My call: revert to design_handoff's more conservative position — iOS 17 floor, `matchedGeometryEffect` as the real, tested implementation — because it was the reasoned decision with an explicit tradeoff written down, versus tech_specs' version which reads like an oversight. If someone wants iOS 18 deliberately, that's a fine call too, but it has to be made on purpose in this gate, not carried forward by accident.

Second, smaller one: ios_architecture_review's persistence section says the App Group entitlement goes on "the main app, widget extension, and intent extension targets identically" — implying three targets — while every phase after it (tech_specs' Route notes, project_plan, and task_assignments T-001's acceptance criteria) correctly locked two targets, with `LogQuoteIntent` living in the main app target and no separate intents extension. This one's already been overwritten correctly downstream, but I want it named explicitly in this gate's output so nobody building T-018 "helpfully" creates a third target because an earlier phase mentioned one.

Now the bigger issue, and this is the one I think actually matters for whether this phase can honestly say "ready": a meaningful slice of this plan's acceptance criteria are real-device-only by design — the 20-photo OCR spike (T-004), camera-denial verified via the actual Settings toggle (T-015), `canSendText() == false` verified on a physical device since Simulator always reports false (T-016), and widgets/intent rendering real data on-device (T-007, T-018, T-023). All of that is the right engineering call, but it assumes a human with a physical iPhone somewhere in the loop. If this build is going to be produced end-to-end by an agent in this environment, none of those can literally be executed as written, and the single worst outcome here is a final acceptance checklist that quietly claims "verified on real device" when nothing of the sort happened. I'd rather name that now than have it surface as a credibility problem at the review gate. My proposed scope adjustment: everything that's testable in a simulator or via unit/fixture tests gets built and actually verified now (OCR fixture tests against sample photographed-text images run through `VNRecognizeTextRequest` directly, the fallback-path behavior of `canSendText()==false` which Simulator actually proves by default, denied-permission code paths exercised via injected authorization state rather than the real Settings app). Everything that is irreducibly real-device-only gets shipped as correct, seam-tested code plus an explicit, separate "Owner Device Verification Checklist" artifact — a named, honest to-do list handed to whoever has a physical iPhone, not folded silently into "done." That's a downgrade of the verification claim, not the code — the acceptance-checklist task (T-023) should be rewritten to distinguish "verified by this build pass" from "requires human device verification before ship," rather than presenting one flat checklist.

On the visionary side: everything else here is unusually well-specified for a pre-code gate — five phases in a row converged without real fights, which is a good sign but also means the risk isn't scope ambiguity anymore, it's execution flatness. The one place I'd push harder than anyone has so far is the Entry Detail artifact and the wrap-up share surface — the product brief called the wrap-up/quote-card the primary acquisition surface, but in the actual task backlog it shows up only as a should-have stats screen (T-013) with no dedicated shareable-image task at all. If the growth thesis is "the artifact is good enough that someone screenshots it," that deserves at least one concrete task with its own acceptance criterion (e.g., Entry Detail renders correctly at share-image aspect ratios, or a lightweight render-to-image affordance), not just a hope that the pull-quote layout happens to look nice. I don't think this should block the build — it's legitimately a could-have — but I'd flag it explicitly as the one spot where "smaller finished app" logic is currently cutting the feature the product brief called out as the standout growth lever, and that tradeoff should be a named, conscious choice in this gate's output, not a silent casualty of task-list granularity.

Everything else checks out clean on a pass through: OCR pipeline (VisionKit→Vision→confirm screen), contacts (picker, no entitlement), sharing (MessageUI + activity fallback), App Group/SwiftData/migration plan, app-lock scenePhase gating, StoreKit-free static paywall, zero third-party dependencies, and the four-lane ownership/branch/merge model are all consistent top-to-bottom across product brief, design, architecture, tech specs, project plan, and tasks — no other contradictions survived the trace. Testing ownership is explicit per lane already, and every major workflow (log book, manual quote, OCR quote, rate/reflect, circle invite via Messages, timeline/stats) has at least one concrete acceptance criterion attached to a task. So the readiness gap here is narrow: one motion-API contradiction to resolve, one stale three-target phrase to formally retire, one honest verification-scope downgrade to write down, and one optional but named growth-feature tradeoff to decide consciously rather than by omission.

Claude is right about one thing that matters a lot: the iOS floor drift is real, and if we leave it ambiguous the build will fork at the navigation layer on day one. Reopening that to restore `matchedGeometryEffect` means rewriting accepted task contracts and reintroducing a second motion implementation right before code. That is exactly the kind of “clean in theory, messy in build” move this gate should reject. So I want us to close this hard: minimum deployment target is iOS 18, zoom transition is the sole implementation, and every earlier iOS 17 / fallback reference is formally superseded. The reason is not “18 is prettier,” it’s that one navigation contract beats two, and nothing else in scope benefits enough from 17 to justify carrying dual-path detail-view motion through build and test.

On the rest, I think Claude’s verification point is correct and needs to become policy, not a note. Right now the docs mix “must be verified on a real device before ship” with “can be verified by this agent build pass,” and those are not the same claim. We should split them explicitly. That means T-004, the Settings-based camera denial check in T-015, the true Messages path in T-016, and on-device widget/intent validation in T-007/T-018/T-023 cannot be silently marked done by simulator or fixture coverage. They need a separate “owner-device verification required” status. That’s not a scope cut to the app; it’s a truth-in-verification fix to the plan.

I also want to lock the two quieter issues from my first round because they still haven’t been contradicted. First, the monetization line needs one canonical sentence and then everyone stops improvising: free includes unlimited books, unlimited entries, manual quotes, stats, app lock, widgets, App Intent, and one circle; paid in v1 is represented only by UI gating for OCR capture and additional circles, with no live billing. If that sentence is not written into the final gate output, somebody will gate the wrong retention feature later because the original spec and later refinements point in slightly different directions. Second, force-quit/relaunch responsibility gets split cleanly: `data_domain` owns automated on-disk persistence tests; `primary_ui` owns manual end-to-end relaunch verification through the actual UI flow. Same theme as the real-device issue: one phrase, two different checks, and they should stop being conflated.

The stale “three targets” language in the architecture review should also be retired explicitly. The settled build is two targets sharing the App Group store: main app and widget. `LogQuoteIntent` lives in the main app target. No intents extension exists. That’s already what the tasks say, but it needs to be named as the final architecture rule so nobody resurrects a third target because of one older paragraph.

On Claude’s growth concern, I think he’s right that the wrap-up/shareable-artifact angle is underrepresented in tasks, but I would not let that reopen scope. The correct close here is to name it as a deliberate non-blocking cut: Entry Detail must already be screenshot-worthy as the shareable artifact, but a dedicated wrap-up image/export surface is deferred out of this build. That keeps the product brief honest without inventing another feature block right before implementation.

So my readiness call is: build-ready once we write down these exact fixes, no further discussion. Lock iOS 18 and zoom-only. Split verification into build-verifiable versus owner-device-only. Lock the free/paid sentence. Split automated persistence versus manual relaunch ownership. Retire the phantom intents extension. Name wrap-up export as deferred, not forgotten. After that, there is no meaningful product, design, or client-architecture ambiguity left; the team can start coding without inventing behavior mid-build.

## Final Output

**Readiness verdict:** Ready to build, with the fixes below written into the record as this gate's authoritative corrections to the plan. No further planning rounds needed.

**Cross-phase consistency check — contradictions found and resolved:**
1. Motion API conflict (design_handoff said iOS 17/`matchedGeometryEffect` baseline; tech_specs and T-012 silently locked iOS 18/zoom-only). **Resolved: iOS 18 is the deployment floor, `.navigationTransition(.zoom(sourceID:in:))` is the sole implementation, no `matchedGeometryEffect` fallback anywhere in the codebase.** Reason: the iOS 17 choice was justified by device-reach concerns that have since expired — iOS 18 has been shipping since September 2024, and by July 2026 the addressable population below it is negligible. One navigation contract, not two.
2. Stale "three targets" language in ios_architecture_review vs. the two-target decision used everywhere downstream. **Resolved: exactly two targets — main app and widget extension — share the App Group store. `LogQuoteIntent` lives in the main app target. No separate intents extension exists, ever.**
3. Free/paid boundary scattered across multiple phases with slightly different phrasing. **Resolved to one canonical sentence, to be copy-pasted verbatim (not paraphrased) into T-020 and T-023:** "Free includes unlimited books, unlimited entries, manual quotes, stats, app lock, widgets, and App Intent, plus one circle; paid in v1 is represented only by UI-only gating on OCR capture and additional circles, with no live billing."
4. Force-quit/relaunch test responsibility ambiguously duplicated between lanes. **Resolved: `data_domain` owns the automated on-disk persistence test (store survives a simulated relaunch); `primary_ui` owns a separate manual end-to-end relaunch walkthrough through the real UI flow.** These are two distinct checks, not one check claimed by two owners.

**Build blockers and fixes:** all four items above are the blockers for this gate, and all four now have a written fix. No blocker remains open.

**Final scope for build:** unchanged from project_plan/task_assignments, with the four fixes above layered in, plus: Entry Detail (T-012) gets one additional acceptance line — layout verified legible at 9:16 and 1:1 share/screenshot crops — since the product brief calls this screen the primary growth artifact and it shouldn't ship untested at anything but standard portrait scroll. The dedicated wrap-up/export image feature stays explicitly deferred as a named, conscious cut, not a silent one.

**Acceptance criteria checklist — now explicitly two-tier, per T-023:**
- **Build-verified tier** (must be actually exercised and passing before this build pass calls itself done): all unit/integration tests (persistence, cascade rules, `ShareEvent` round-trip, streak/date matrix, migration test, perf gate), simulator-level UI flows (manual + OCR-fake compose convergence, timeline/stats states, zoom-transition tap-row-to-detail-and-back smoke test), OCR fixture tests against sample photographed-text images run directly through `VNRecognizeTextRequest`, app-lock scenePhase state-machine logic (pure and fully unit-testable without a device), and `canSendText()==false` fallback behavior (this is Simulator's actual default, so it's genuinely covered here, not deferred).
- **Owner Device Verification Required tier** (ships as its own named deliverable document, never silently folded into "done"): the 20-real-book OCR spike (T-004) run through the live VisionKit/Vision pipeline, camera-permission denial verified via the actual Settings toggle (T-015), the true MFMessageComposeViewController send path (T-016) which Simulator cannot exercise, and widgets/App Intent rendering real persisted data on physical hardware (T-007/T-018/T-023).

Both tiers must appear as separate, explicitly labeled sections in the final acceptance-checklist artifact — a flat, undifferentiated list that implies real-device work happened when it didn't is treated as a defect in the deliverable itself.
